key_constructs.purpose: Queues requests that require a robots.txt lookup and issues a single robots fetch, appending waiting requests into a per-robots-url queue.
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements the optimization 'fetch robots once, then replay' — editing this must preserve the pending_robots_queue semantics and the callback chain (robotsHeaderCallback/data/done/error/shutdown).

key_constructs.name: _abort
key_constructs.purpose: Internal helper to abort either all active transfers or transfers belonging to a specific frame_id; used by abort and abortFrame.
key_constructs.reasoning: Contains logic that distinguishes transports (http vs websocket) and uses different kill logic; must not be refactored in ways that break transport checks or counted invariants (active, intercepted).

key_behaviors: queues requests when no connection available
serves GET requests from cache when valid
fetches robots.txt once per host and queues dependent requests
supports request interception and blocking semantics for CDP
aborts transfers per-frame or globally

pitfalls.mistake: Calling transfer.deinit() or freeing transfer resources directly from inside libcurl callbacks as if synchronous
pitfalls.consequence: Use-after-free or crashes because libcurl may still reference the easy handle or other resources during callbacks
pitfalls.prevention: Respect existing kill() semantics: if client.performing is true, mark aborted and replace callbacks with no-ops; real deinit happens outside callback context

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming Transfer.fromConnection always yields a valid Transfer pointer and casting without checking transport
pitfalls.consequence: When Connection.transport is a websocket or .none, a blind cast will read incorrect memory or crash
pitfalls.prevention: Switch on conn.transport and handle .http and .websocket cases explicitly; follow processMessages/processOneMessage patterns

pitfalls.mistake: Assigning header slices into long-lived cache metadata before ensuring their backing buffer lifetime
pitfalls.consequence: Header slices can point to moved/freed memory, causing corrupted cache entries or crashes later during cache.put/get
pitfalls.prevention: Allocate pending CachedMetadata on the arena and ensure headers/vary_headers are collected/assigned before any move/clear of temporary header_list; follow commit reorderings described in history

pitfalls.mistake: Invoking req.done_callback before completing cache.put and releasing connection
pitfalls.consequence: Callback may re-enter and rely on cache state that hasn't been finalized, producing races or inconsistent state
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure cache.put runs and completes before calling transfer.releaseConn() and done_callback (see commit history ordering)

pitfalls.mistake: Changing http_proxy or proxy usage while connections are in-flight without ensuring no active connections
pitfalls.consequence: Libcurl may document undefined behavior for changing certain options during a connection lifecycle; could lead to undefined network behavior
pitfalls.prevention: Respect changeProxy's ensureNoActiveConnection restriction: only change proxy configuration when no in-use connections exist

key_sections: Client: definition and important fields (handles, queue, in_use, transfer_pool, max_response_size)
processRequest: cache fast path, intercept handling and Transfer creation
makeRequest/process/processMessages/processOneMessage: the perform loop and message handling (search for these later in file)
abort/_abort: per-frame vs global abort semantics
skip_unless_needed: low-level robots* callbacks (robotsHeaderCallback, robotsDataCallback) unless modifying robots flow
small helpers and trivial getters (newHeaders) unless header construction changes are planned

tests.example_command: zig build test or make test (repository top-level)

related_files.relationship: Provides low-level libcurl binding, Connection/Easy abstractions and Header/Curl iteration used by Client to perform transfers
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change to how Client configures curl (proxy, tls_verify, headers) or processes curl messages must be reflected in network/http.zig; also Connection.transport semantics are defined there

related_files.relationship: Cache API used by Client for get/put and cacheability decisions (tryCache); Client must honor CacheRequest/CachedMetadata contracts
related_files.reason_to_check: Cache key formats, header formats and CachedMetadata layout must match what Client writes/reads

related_files.relationship: Script loading uses Client to fetch scripts and depends on interception/blocking semantics and header construction
related_files.reason_to_check: ScriptManager relies on Client.newHeaders and client behavior when scripts are blocking vs non-blocking

related_files.relationship: Page navigations create Requests handled by Client; Page expects certain notifications and ordering (header_done before header callback, parse state changes)
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change in event ordering or Response API will affect Page lifecycle and CDP event emission

insights.title: Track connections via trackConn/removeConn instead of old private-from-conn approach
insights.problem: Code relied on getting Transfer from curl private pointer and in several places used fromConnection() which became fragile when Connection.transport changed.
insights.root_cause: Connection private storage was reset by curl and transport changes required a different tracking approach.
insights.solution: Introduce trackConn() which sets conn private pointer and adds to handles; use removeConn() to reverse — make transfers get connection via msg.conn.transport.http in callbacks.
insights.lesson_learned: When integrating with C libraries that mutate state (curl_easy_reset), set per-use private pointers every time and centralize connection bookkeeping to avoid racey / stale pointers.

insights.title: Avoid using Transfer-specific fields for websocket transport and check transport before casting
insights.problem: Code assumed Transfer.fromConnection always valid; with websockets stored in Connection.transport this assumption could crash or read wrong memory.
insights.root_cause: Mixing HTTP Transfer and WebSocket connection handling without distinguishing transports.
insights.solution: Switch on conn.transport when processing messages to handle .http and .websocket cases separately; remove fromConnection helper and use conn.transport.http.
insights.lesson_learned: Always branch on tagged unions before casting; when adding new transport types, update message-processing logic to be transport-aware.

insights.title: Add max_response_size into client and enforce client-level limit consistently
insights.problem: Transfer-level max_response_size handling was inconsistent/missing after refactors; response-too-large checks needed central client config.
insights.root_cause: Multiple places referenced transfer.max_response_size; client holds network.config but field usage changed.
insights.solution: Store max_response_size on client from network.config and use transfer.client.max_response_size for checks; remove transfer-level duplication.
insights.lesson_learned: Centralize configuration at client/network level and reference it from transfers to avoid duplicated config state and missed updates.

insights.title: Allocate CacheMetadata on use to avoid storing stack/local structures
insights.problem: Pending cache metadata stored by value caused lifecycle issues (overwriting memory) when used asynchronously.
insights.root_cause: Transfer._pending_cache_metadata previously held a value; when processing later the stack/arena could be invalid.
insights.solution: Allocate CacheMetadata on arena/allocator and store pointer in _pending_cache_metadata; update cache.put usage to dereference pointer.
insights.lesson_learned: Async bookkeeping items must be heap/arena allocated if their lifetime extends beyond the local function; don't store stack structs for deferred operations.

insights.title: Assign cache header slices before moving/clearing temporaries
insights.problem: Cached metadata stored pointers into a header_list that could be moved/invalidated causing stale/invalid header slices.
insights.root_cause: Header collection (header_list) lifetime and assignments to transfer._pending_cache_metadata were ordered such that the header slices could point to memory that was moved or later changed.
insights.solution: Reordered code so headers and vary_headers are assigned into _pending_cache_metadata before any potential move/clear of the header_list; ensure header collection is done while the allocator arena is still valid.
insights.lesson_learned: When storing slices into long-lived structs, ensure the underlying buffers' ownership/lifetime are stable and assign them before any operation that might move/free backing storage.

insights.title: Put cache entry before releasing connection and invoking callbacks
insights.problem: Connections were being released and done callbacks run before the cache.put completed. Those callbacks can trigger other loads that may race with the cache put or rely on cache state.
insights.root_cause: Releasing the connection early (transfer.releaseConn()) and invoking done_callback before putting into cache created a timing window where other code could run and interact with cache/transfer state.
insights.solution: Move cache.put (and its logging) so it runs before calling transfer.releaseConn() and before calling done_callback; then call releaseConn() and done_callback last.
insights.lesson_learned: When a completion callback may trigger new asynchronous operations, finalize shared state (like caching) before releasing shared resources or invoking callbacks.

insights.title: Fix incorrect ctx passed to shutdown callback
insights.problem: shutdown callback was invoked with the wrong context pointer (self.ctx instead of self.req.ctx).
insights.root_cause: Typo/incorrect field used when calling the shutdown callback.
insights.solution: Use cb(self.req.ctx) instead of cb(self.ctx).
insights.lesson_learned: Callbacks that accept a ctx should consistently use the request's ctx; small typos can lead to invalid pointer/corrupted context behavior.

insights.title: Collect request headers only when caching is possible
insights.problem: Previously headers were duplicated/collected even when the response would not be cacheable, wasting allocations.
insights.root_cause: Header collection was done prior to evaluating cacheability.
insights.solution: Call Cache.tryCache early to determine if caching is possible; only when caching will occur, collect and dup headers into arena and set _pending_cache_metadata.
insights.lesson_learned: Short-circuit expensive allocations by checking whether the work will be needed (cacheability checks first). Use a pending metadata field to avoid storing until ready.

insights.title: Always close file handles when serving from cache
insights.problem: Cached file responses were not closed on all exit paths, leaking file descriptors.
insights.root_cause: File closing logic existed in some branches but not consistently across all early returns and success paths.
insights.solution: Use defer and explicit switch branches to close file resources in all code paths (defer closing the file, and close on error/early return paths).
insights.lesson_learned: When returning file-backed resources to callers, ensure all early-return/error branches properly close file handles to avoid FD leaks; prefer defer for clarity.

insights.title: Adapt to CacheRequest and expose richer cache key data
insights.problem: Cache interface originally used only URL strings; Vary and expiration semantics required more request context (timestamp, request headers).
insights.root_cause: Cache API was too simplistic to support Vary and deterministic expiry/testing.
insights.solution: Switch to CacheRequest objects (and later extended to include timestamp and request headers) and update usages across the client to pass arena from an arena_pool for cache ops.
insights.lesson_learned: Cache APIs should accept a structured request object to support advanced matching rules (Vary), deterministic expiry (timestamp), and additional request metadata.

insights.title: Use Response wrapper in callbacks instead of Transfer
insights.problem: Callbacks exposed internal Transfer pointer which made it hard to represent cached responses or abstract live vs cached sources and complicated lifecycle management.
insights.root_cause: Handlers needed a single abstraction to represent either a live Transfer or a CachedResponse; previously the API tied callbacks to Transfer only.
insights.solution: Introduce Response struct (fromTransfer/fromCached) and change Request callbacks signatures to accept Response. Update all call sites to use Response and adapt response methods (status, contentType, contentLength, url, headerIterator) to support cached variant.
insights.lesson_learned: Design callback APIs around stable, abstract response objects rather than internal transfer structs so different sources (network, cache) can be unified.

insights.title: Add basic HTTP caching integration
insights.problem: No caching existed; repeated requests fetched content over network.
insights.root_cause: Caching not implemented previously.
insights.solution: Add cache.get in processRequest for GET requests, serveFromCache to replay cached responses, and cache.put in processOneMessage to store responses. Collect response headers into header_list to build CachedMetadata via fromHeaders.
insights.lesson_learned: When integrating cache: gather all headers (for cache decisions), ensure response body is buffered/available for caching, and keep cache key strategy explicit (TODO: Vary keying).

insights.title: Relaxed assertions during client abort
insights.problem: Hard assertion that no active transfers remained after abort_all could fail if transfers exist but are in aborted state.
insights.root_cause: Assumption that abort_all leaves zero transfers was brittle under concurrency/timing.
insights.solution: Replace the assertion with a loop asserting that any remaining transfers are flagged aborted and assert active == leftover; keep invariants but avoid false positives.
insights.lesson_learned: Avoid brittle global invariants in async code; assert state conditions (e.g., 'all remaining transfers are aborted') rather than exact counts where concurrency/timing may vary.

insights.title: Use transfer.req.ctx consistently for callbacks
insights.problem: Some code used inconsistent ctx references (transfer.ctx vs req.ctx) causing incorrect contexts passed to callbacks.
insights.root_cause: Transfer kept a ctx field but code evolution changed where ctx should be sourced from; refactor to use Response.ctx or req.ctx consistently.
insights.solution: Centralize on Response.fromTransfer to set ctx = transfer.req.ctx and replace callback calls to use Response, and ensure when invoking done/error callbacks we pass req.ctx.

insights.title: Use Network type name for dependency injection
insights.problem: HttpClient used Runtime type name in fields and signatures.
insights.root_cause: Callers and types needed to be updated after Runtime->Network rename.
insights.solution: Change field name and type to network: *Network and update init signature (commit 0604056).
insights.lesson_learned: When renaming lower-layer modules, update all public/private signatures to maintain consistent naming and avoid confusion.

insights.title: Dispatch http_response_header_done before header callback
insights.problem: Header-handling ordering caused page navigation and CDP events to be emitted at times that didn't match Chrome's ordering.
insights.root_cause: The header callback invocation and notification dispatch order were suboptimal; page header completion should dispatch the notification earlier to allow CDP events to reflect header/commit timing.
insights.solution: Move transfer.req.notification.dispatch(.http_response_header_done, ...) to just after header parsing and before calling header_callback (commit 752184b).
insights.lesson_learned: Event emission order matters for protocol compatibility; ensure internal notifications are dispatched at the same logical commit points as upstream implementations (e.g., response header commit).

insights.title: Protect Transfer.kill() from being executed inside libcurl callbacks
insights.problem: Calling transfer.kill() from inside libcurl callbacks could deinit the transfer while callbacks were still running, causing crashes (use-after-free).
insights.root_cause: transfer.kill() previously deinitialized the transfer unconditionally which is unsafe when invoked from within libcurl callbacks; libcurl does not expect the easy handle's resources to be mutated during callbacks.
insights.solution: If the transfer or client is currently performing (inside callbacks), kill() now marks aborted = true and replaces all callbacks with no-op handlers instead of deiniting the transfer synchronously. Otherwise deinit() is performed.
insights.lesson_learned: Mutating or deinitializing objects from inside C callbacks is unsafe. Prefer deferring deinit or neutralizing callbacks and mark state so actual cleanup can occur later outside callback context.

insights.title: Detect auth challenges from CONNECT response headers
insights.problem: Proxy authentication challenges during CONNECT may not have been recognized because only response headers were checked.
insights.root_cause: Auth challenge detection only inspected response headers and response code; CONNECT-phase headers (from curl) and connect status code were ignored.
insights.solution: detectAuthChallenge now also checks conn.getConnectHeader("WWW-Authenticate"/"Proxy-Authenticate") and conn.getConnectCode(), and sets _auth_challenge accordingly.
insights.lesson_learned: For proxied/CONNECT flows, some servers return auth challenges in the CONNECT response; HTTP client must inspect both response and connect headers/status. When integrating with libcurl, use connect-specific info getters.

insights.title: Preserve auth challenge across transfer.reset for retries
insights.problem: Retries called transfer.reset() which cleared the per-transfer auth challenge, losing state needed to decide credential application for proxy vs server.
insights.root_cause: Transfer.reset cleared _auth_challenge unconditionally; makeRequest needed that info preserved across reset/retries.
insights.solution: When preparing for retry, save transfer._auth_challenge, call transfer.reset(), then restore _auth_challenge.
insights.lesson_learned: When resetting per-request state for retries, selectively preserve data that crosses retry boundaries (e.g., auth challenge). Transfer.reset should not blindly drop state required by higher-level logic.

insights.title: Robust error handling & separation of message processing logic
insights.problem: processMessages mixed callback replay, error logging, redirect handling and connection cleanup in an ad-hoc way; errors during processing could leave connections orphaned or cause double-free attempts.
insights.root_cause: The original inline processMessages function performed many responsibilities and used continue/break control flow with errors handled inconsistently.
insights.solution: Refactored to processOneMessage that returns done/continue signal; created clearer error path where processMessages calls processOneMessage and centrally handles errors, logs, requestFailed, and cleans up detached connections. Introduced _detached_conn to track temporarily detached connections during redirect reconfiguration and improved deinit/cleanup semantics.
insights.lesson_learned: Complex I/O state machines benefit from splitting into smaller functions that return explicit status rather than mixing control flow with many continues/breaks. Track transient resources (detached connections) explicitly to avoid double-free or leaked handles.

insights.title: Buffer response body for callback replay and move CDP callbacks to process stage
insights.problem: Callbacks invoked directly from libcurl's write callback caused re-entrancy and ordering issues (CDP notifications invoked from dataCallback). Data and header callbacks were executing at different times without a single consistent place to handle client notifications and errors.
insights.root_cause: Earlier code called user data callbacks and CDP notifications inside the libcurl data callback; this made error handling and ordering difficult.
insights.solution: Buffer response body in transfer._stream_buffer and defer calling req.data_callback and CDP notifications until processMessages/processOneMessage. Move cookie extraction, header_done and response-data notifications to processing phase where errors can be handled uniformly.
insights.lesson_learned: Avoid calling high-level callbacks directly from low-level I/O callbacks. Buffering and replaying callbacks in a controlled processing phase simplifies error handling and ensures consistent ordering for protocol notifications.

insights.title: Skip body handling for responses that trigger retries (redirects, 401/407)
insights.problem: Data callback attempted to read body for responses that should be retried, leading to extra work and wrong behavior when a redirect / auth challenge was signaled by initial headers.
insights.root_cause: Header handling and write/data callbacks weren't coordinating: body could arrive before processing determined a retry case.
insights.solution: On first data chunk, check response code; if it's a redirect or auth-challenge status, set _skip_body and return error to stop curl's write handling. Subsequent logic skips body chunks when _skip_body is set.
insights.lesson_learned: I/O callback code must consider that headers and body may arrive interleaved; detect retry-worthy statuses early and avoid processing body data when the transfer will be retried.

insights.title: Cookie origin propagation and centralized cookie handling
insights.problem: Cookies were being managed in multiple places with ad-hoc headers and RequestCookie structure; cookie origin (used for SameSite decisions) was not consistently passed to outgoing requests.
insights.root_cause: Request creation points (navigate, script loads, fetch/xhr) did not pass the request origin into the underlying Request/Transfer; cookie jar APIs expect an origin_url parameter to evaluate SameSite attributes.
insights.solution: Added cookie_origin field to Request and propagated page.url into requests in Page.navigate, ScriptManager.*, Fetch.init and XHR.send. Centralized cookie header generation in Transfer/request send path using jar.forRequest with origin_url = req.cookie_origin. Removed older RequestCookie abstraction.
insights.lesson_learned: Cookie semantics like SameSite depend on the request's origin context; ensure origin is passed down when creating network requests and centralize cookie header generation to avoid inconsistent behavior.

insights.title: Connection lifecycle and libcurl 'easy' handle cleanup improvements
insights.problem: Connection reset/cleanup and multi-handle management had subtle inconsistencies: earlier code used different fields (easy vs _easy) and reset semantics were scattered.
insights.root_cause: Connection abstraction evolved; some code still referenced .easy while others used updated ._easy; reset and deinit semantics required additional context (config, ca_blob).
insights.solution: Standardized on Connection._easy field, added Connection.reset(config, ca_blob) to reconfigure easy handle, added curl_easy_reset wrapper in sys/libcurl.zig, updated all call sites to use _easy, ensured reset is invoked with runtime configuration when releasing connections.
insights.lesson_learned: When refactoring low-level C bindings wrappers it's critical to update all call sites consistently and provide reset functions that accept runtime config so released connections are reinitialized correctly before reuse.

insights.title: Detect auth challenge also on RecvError message
insights.problem: Proxy could send 407 with headers then close the connection; code only checked msg.err == null to detect auth challenge, missing cases where libcurl reports a RecvError but headers were included.
insights.root_cause: Process message condition only inspected absence of error; RecvError could still have headers to parse.
insights.solution: Treat msg.err == null OR msg.err == error.RecvError as a case to detectAuthChallenge.
insights.lesson_learned: I/O error enums may include error values that carry usable headers/data — don't assume any non-null error means absence of response headers.

insights.title: Treat TLS connection close after 'Connection: close' as successful end-of-body
insights.problem: Some servers close the TCP connection without sending a TLS close_notify after responding with Connection: close. BoringSSL reports this as RecvError which libcurl surfaces as CURLE_RECV_ERROR, causing the client to treat an otherwise valid response as a fatal error.
insights.root_cause: The TLS library reports missing close_notify as a receive error; code didn't special-case this when valid HTTP headers were already received and server indicated connection close semantics.
insights.solution: Detect CURLE_RECV_ERROR when header parsing was already completed and the response header 'connection' equals 'close'; treat that condition as success (ignore the error) instead of failing the request.

src/browser/Page.zig.short_description: Represents a document/frame: DOM container, lifecycle, navigation, and ties to ScriptManager, StyleManager and HTTP requests.
src/browser/Page.zig.description:
Page.zig defines the Page type which models a browsing context (root page or iframe). It owns DOM objects (Document, Window), manages lifecycle state (parse/load state, navigation queue), integrates with the JavaScript engine (JS.Context), and coordinates network-related actions (navigation requests, headers for scripts/XHR/fetch). The Page is designed with arenas and an explicit reference-counting model: many WebAPI objects are allocated in a page arena and explicitly released on deinit. Page also contains many lazily-initialized lookup maps (attribute lookup, classList, dataset) to reduce per-element memory overhead.

Page.init wires together the factory-produced Document, creates Window, initializes StyleManager and ScriptManager, and creates a V8/JS context via the Browser environment. Page.deinit carefully releases all retained references (blobs, observers, selection), shuts down the ScriptManager, and aborts network activity either globally or per-frame via browser.http_client.abort()/abortFrame(). The file encodes many lifecycle invariants: the parse_state and load_state drive event ordering, pending load counters are used for firing window.load, and queued navigation objects live in arenas that must be released when navigation completes or is abandoned.

The page coordinates with HttpClient: headersForRequest builds referer header cache and expects HttpClient to handle cookie insertion (cookie_origin must be set on requests). There are several historically-motivated fixes in this module visible through comments and commit insights: isSameOrigin was hardened to avoid host prefix collisions, cookie_origin propagation was added to ensure SameSite cookie semantics, and deinit was updated to explicitly release reference-counted objects. When editing Page, be mindful of arena lifetimes, releaseRef pairing, and the ordering of state transitions that callers (ScriptManager, CDP events) observe.

key_constructs.name: Page
key_constructs.purpose: Primary struct representing a browsing context (frame/root) and containing DOM, JS context, managers and state.
key_constructs.reasoning: Most page-level operations (navigation, DOM mutation, scripting) are methods on Page; changes to its layout affect many higher-level subsystems.

key_constructs.name: init
key_constructs.purpose: Initializes Page: sets up document/window, style/script managers, JS context, and scheduling for idle tasks for root pages.
key_constructs.reasoning: Entry point for creating new pages/frames; it depends on Session and Browser environment APIs. Ordering (create Document, Window, then ScriptManager, then JS context) matters for object wiring and identity maps.

key_constructs.name: deinit
key_constructs.purpose: Tears down Page: deinitializes frames, releases reference-counted objects, aborts relevant network transfers, and destroys JS context and managers.
key_constructs.reasoning: Deinit must carefully release resources in the right order to avoid leaks and avoid calling into already-freed arenas/contexts; per-frame vs root behavior differs (abortFrame vs abort).

key_constructs.name: headersForRequest
key_constructs.purpose: Adds common headers to outgoing requests from this page, notably a cached Referer header built from the page's url.
key_constructs.reasoning: This helper is called by ScriptManager and other network request builders; it caches a referer_header string in the page arena and intentionally does not manage cookies (those are handled downstream by HttpClient).

key_constructs.name: isSameOrigin
key_constructs.purpose: Determines same-origin relationship between the page's current origin and a given URL; uses a fast startsWith path and then strict host comparison.
key_constructs.reasoning: Security-sensitive function used by cross-origin checks; commit history shows this was hardened to avoid prefix collisions (e.g., origin.com vs origin.com.attacker.com), so edits must preserve full host:port equality semantics.

key_constructs.name: navigate
key_constructs.purpose: Initiates a navigation: constructs Request objects, handles special URLs (about:blank, blob:) and delegates network fetch to HttpClient; sets page parse state and schedules script manager tasks.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is a major flow tying Page to HttpClient and ScriptManager. It sets cookie_origin, base_url, parse state, and clears/sets various lifecycle counters — changes must preserve ordering and side effects expected by CDP and ScriptManager.

key_constructs.name: getArena
key_constructs.purpose: Proxy to Session.getArena to obtain temporary arena allocators used for short-lived allocations during DOM/script operations.
key_constructs.reasoning: Arenas are a central allocation pattern; misuse or incorrect release of arenas leads to memory corruption or leaks. Callers should use getArena/releaseArena symmetrically.

key_constructs.name: deinit (releaseRef sequences)
key_constructs.purpose: Block inside deinit that iterates blob_urls, observers, and other RC types and calls releaseRef(session) for each.
key_constructs.reasoning: This explicit releaseRef pattern replaced finalizer-based cleanup; modifications must keep the acquire/release balance in mind to avoid double-release or leaked refs.

key_behaviors: creates and initializes JS contexts and DOM for new pages
orchestrates navigation including about:blank and blob: URLs
propagates cookie_origin into network Requests
manages page parse/load state and fires load-related tasks
releases page-scoped resources and aborts network transfers on deinit

pitfalls.mistake: Allocating transient data in an arena and not releasing it with releaseArena, or releasing it too early
pitfalls.consequence: Use-after-free or corrupted allocations when other parts of the page expect the arena to remain valid during a call
pitfalls.prevention: Use getArena/releaseArena symmetrically; check errdefer patterns used in init and functions like addFromElement in ScriptManager

pitfalls.mistake: Calling session.browser.http_client.abort() for a frame deinit when the code intends a per-frame abort
pitfalls.consequence: Overly broad abort cancels unrelated requests and may impact other pages (the client is shared)
pitfalls.prevention: Respect parent==null semantics: root page uses abort(), frames use abortFrame(self._frame_id); do not change this logic

pitfalls.mistake: Changing isSameOrigin logic to rely only on startsWith or only on prefix checks
pitfalls.consequence: Allows origin spoofing where a malicious origin with the original as a prefix bypasses origin checks
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the two-stage check: startsWith for fast-path then strict host comparison via URL.getHost for correctness

pitfalls.mistake: Releasing references (releaseRef) on objects that were never acquired or in early return paths
pitfalls.consequence: Double-release or release of unowned objects leading to undefined behavior
pitfalls.prevention: Follow commit history fixes: ensure early returns occur before acquiring refs or ensure symmetric acquire/release

key_sections: init: wiring of Document, Window, ScriptManager and JS context (establishes crucial object graph)
navigate: how Requests are created and cookie_origin is set for HttpClient integration
deinit: exact order of resource release, JS context destruction and network abort behavior
headersForRequest & isSameOrigin: small but security-sensitive utilities
skip_unless_needed: the many lazy lookup maps (attribute lookup, dataset, style lookups) unless editing per-element memory optimizations
internal mutation/observer scheduling fields unless changing mutation observer semantics

tests.example_command: zig build test or make test (repository top-level)

related_files.relationship: Script loading/execution integrated with Page lifecycle; ScriptManager uses Page.headersForRequest and the HttpClient instance attached to Browser
related_files.reason_to_check: If altering how Page sets referer/cookie_origin or navigation ordering, ScriptManager behavior (script execution, blocking vs async) will be affected

related_files.relationship: Page uses HttpClient for all network requests, including navigate, XHR and script fetch; relies on Response wrapper and notification ordering
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes in Response API or event ordering (header_done vs header_callback) impact Page's parsing and CDP event emission

related_files.relationship: XHR is implemented on top of HttpClient and is a direct consumer of Page semantics (arena, releaseRef, response handling)
related_files.reason_to_check: If altering Page cookie_origin or headersForRequest, XHR SameSite behaviors and header attachments need verification

tests_to_run: run relevant end-to-end navigation tests (CI/demo) if changing navigation lifecycles
data_constants_to_check: Session.arena_pool debug tags if allocating arenas
Network config values used by Page for navigation (cookie semantics)

insights.title: Don't release an un-acquired reference on CDP input events
insights.problem: Code released an event reference even when there was no active element to receive it, leading to release of an un-acquired reference.
insights.root_cause: Early return path released event via event.releaseRef(self._session) even though the reference had not been acquired for the page context.
insights.solution: Simplify early return to return immediately if no active element is present; avoid releasing the event in that path.
insights.lesson_learned: When manipulating ref-counted objects, ensure ownership assumptions are clear on all return paths; prefer early returns before acquiring refs or ensure symmetric acquire/release.

insights.title: Use Response abstraction in page HTTP callbacks
insights.problem: Page callbacks used Transfer pointer; after switching to Response wrapper, these had to be updated to access response metadata consistently.
insights.root_cause: HttpClient changed callback API from Transfer to Response.
insights.solution: Update pageHeaderDoneCallback/pageDataCallback to accept HttpClient.Response, use response.status(), contentType(), url(), contentLength(), etc.
insights.lesson_learned: Centralizing on Response simplifies consumers but requires auditing all call sites for lifecycle and field access differences.

insights.title: Stricter Page.isSameOrigin to avoid host prefix collisions
insights.problem: isSameOrigin used startsWith which allowed 'https://origin.com' to match 'https://origin.com.attacker.com'.
insights.root_cause: startsWith only checks prefix; origin matching must compare host:port exactly (and scheme).
insights.solution: Keep fast-path startsWith but then compare host (domain:port) strictly using URL.getHost on both current_origin and candidate url. Added unit tests covering many variants (different port, subdomain, scheme, etc.).
insights.lesson_learned: Origin comparisons must be strict: host/port equality, not prefix checks. Add tests to cover port and subdomain edge cases.

insights.title: Explicit reference counting on Page deinit
insights.problem: Page held references to Blobs, Observers and other RC types but relied on finalizer fallbacks rather than explicit releases.
insights.root_cause: Finalizer callback infrastructure was used as a catch-all causing implicit/lazy release semantics and potential ownership confusion.
insights.solution: On Page.deinit now explicitly releaseRef on blob_urls, mutation/intersection observers and selection objects to match the move to pure reference counting (commit 77b60ce).
insights.lesson_learned: Finalizers should be a fallback; keep reference counting balanced (acquire/release) in normal control flow to avoid use-after-free and hiding ownership bugs.

insights.title: Adapt to new releaseRef semantics in event flows
insights.problem: Page code previously called deinit(false, session) on events; with RC model this led to mismatches.
insights.root_cause: After finalizer restructure, deinit signature/semantics changed; callers must release refs to decrement counts rather than calling deinit directly.
insights.solution: Replaced direct deinit calls with releaseRef on event objects (e.g., keyboard_event handling, submit_event defer), ensuring proper reference lifecycle.
insights.lesson_learned: When globally changing lifecycle API, update all consumers (event dispatch, form submission, input handling) to use the new acquire/release API to prevent leaks or double-free.

insights.title: Propagate cookie_origin when creating requests
insights.problem: Page.navigate and other request entry points didn't set an explicit cookie origin, causing incorrect SameSite evaluations for cookies.
insights.root_cause: Request creation omitted cookie_origin field; cookie jar API expects origin_url to decide which cookies to attach.
insights.solution: Set .cookie_origin = self.url when building the Request in Page.navigate; other call sites also mirror this pattern.
insights.lesson_learned: All high-level request creators must pass the page/request origin to lower-level network components to satisfy cookie semantics (SameSite, origin-scoped cookies).

insights.title: Mark parse state complete after feeding HTML to parser
insights.problem: An end-to-end test expected parse state to be .complete after pageDoneCallback; it wasn't being set, which could change subsequent lifecycle behavior.
insights.root_cause: pageDoneCallback parsed HTML but did not set the internal _parse_state to .complete before calling documentIsComplete.
insights.solution: Set self._parse_state = .complete before calling documentIsComplete() in pageDoneCallback.
insights.lesson_learned: Explicitly update lifecycle state fields before broadcasting completion events so listeners / subsequent logic see consistent state.

insights.title: Headers building API simplified (removed arena arg)
insights.problem: headersForRequest previously required an allocator argument for cookie/header construction; many callers passed different arenas making usage inconsistent.
insights.root_cause: Earlier cookie/request header code used per-call temporary arenas.
insights.solution: Change headersForRequest signature to (self, headers) without allocator param and centralize cookie insertion into HttpClient.Transfer path. Update ScriptManager, Fetch, and XHR call sites accordingly.
insights.lesson_learned: Simplify call-site contracts by moving temporary allocation concerns into lower-level modules; keep public APIs minimal and stable.

insights.title: requestSubmit(submitter) must set SubmitEvent.submitter
insights.problem: Form.requestSubmit(submitter) created a generic Event (submit) which did not carry submitter information, so event.submitter was unset even when a submitter was provided.
insights.root_cause: Submit event was created as a plain Event lacking the submitter field; there was no SubmitEvent type used, so submitter could not be populated.
insights.solution: Introduce SubmitEvent type and use SubmitEvent.initTrusted to create the submit event with .submitter set; cast to Event for existing handling.

insights.title: Initialize cross-origin wrapper on Page.window
insights.problem: Cross-origin access wrapper needed to be set up when Page is initialized so CrossOriginWindow can be returned from Access.init.
insights.root_cause: Cross-origin wrapper structure wasn't initialized at Page creation time.
insights.solution: Set self.window._cross_origin_wrapper = .{ .window = self.window } during Page.init.

src/cdp/domains/lp.zig.short_description: CDP/LP domain handlers: expose Lightpanda page analysis and action RPCs for CDP/MCP.
src/cdp/domains/lp.zig.description:
This file implements the LP CDP domain message dispatcher and handlers that expose page-analysis and action primitives to remote clients (CDP/MCP). It maps incoming RPC commands (via CDP.Command) to domain-specific operations implemented in the browser modules (markdown, SemanticTree, interactive, forms, structured_data, and actions). The handlers use the current browser context (cmd.browser_context) and the session's current page to operate on DOM nodes, register nodes in the backend registry, and return structured results or errors via cmd.sendResult / early-return error values.

Why it exists: LP domain provides a compact RPC surface to let external clients (agents, tooling, test harnesses) request semantic dumps, markdown, interactive element extraction, node inspection, form detection and DOM-manipulating actions (click/fill/scroll/waitForSelector). The design keeps protocol parsing minimal and delegates heavy lifting to browser modules (SemanticTree, actions, forms, etc.), reusing the node registry to provide stable backend node identifiers. Handlers are careful to convert internal errors into CDP-level error codes.

How it fits: This file is the CDP-facing bridge between incoming message parsing and internal browser capabilities. It relies heavily on bc.node_registry for mapping user-visible backend node ids to DOM nodes. It also uses cmd.arena (an allocator) for temporary allocations when generating string results (e.g., markdown text and semantic tree text dumps). Tests are embedded at the bottom of the file to assert expected behavior of a subset of endpoints (getMarkdown, getInteractiveElements, getStructuredData, action tools, waitForSelector).

key_constructs.name: processMessage
key_constructs.purpose: Dispatch incoming CDP.Command to the appropriate LP action handler.
key_constructs.reasoning: Central entrypoint that must parse cmd.input.action to a compile-time enum and route to the exact handler; callers expect specific error mapping when unknown or invalid actions are received.

key_constructs.name: getSemanticTree
key_constructs.purpose: Return a semantic tree object or its text representation for a document subtree.
key_constructs.reasoning: Builds a SemanticTree instance configured with registry, page, arena and options; may return a JSON object (st) or a text string via an arena-backed allocating writer — care must be taken with arena lifetime and pruning/max_depth semantics.

key_constructs.name: getMarkdown
key_constructs.purpose: Dump node subtree to markdown text and send as RPC result.
key_constructs.reasoning: Uses lp.markdown.dump with cmd.arena via a temporary Allocating writer. It returns markdown bytes in the result; allocation and deinit of the writer must be preserved to avoid leaks and invalid memory.

key_constructs.name: getInteractiveElements
key_constructs.purpose: Collect interactive elements under a root and register them in the backend node registry.
key_constructs.reasoning: Calls interactive.collectInteractiveElements then interactive.registerNodes to populate backendNodeId on element objects; do not duplicate registration elsewhere to avoid inconsistent IDs.

key_constructs.name: getNodeDetails
key_constructs.purpose: Look up a node by backendNodeId and return SemanticTree.getNodeDetails result.
key_constructs.reasoning: Performs registry lookup and delegates to SemanticTree.getNodeDetails; must return error.InvalidNodeId if node missing, and guard against internal errors when serializing details.

key_constructs.name: clickNode
key_constructs.purpose: Perform click action on a registered node and respond to the caller.
key_constructs.reasoning: Looks up the node in bc.node_registry and calls lp.actions.click; maps action-specific errors to CDP error codes (InvalidParam on InvalidNodeType) and returns empty success object when OK.

key_constructs.name: fillNode
key_constructs.purpose: Fill text into an input/select/textarea node by backend id.
key_constructs.reasoning: Parses text param and uses lp.actions.fill; returns InvalidParam when node type is inappropriate; ensure the passed text is not mutated after arena lifetime ends.

key_constructs.name: scrollNode
key_constructs.purpose: Scroll the window or a specific element and emit scroll events.
key_constructs.reasoning: Accepts optional backendNodeId or nodeId (legacy) and optional x/y coordinates; converts node lookup results to nullable DOM node pointer and forwards to lp.actions.scroll which dispatches scroll event.

key_constructs.name: waitForSelector
key_constructs.purpose: Wait for a CSS selector to appear in the page and return backendNodeId of the found element.
key_constructs.reasoning: Duplicates the parse/timeout pattern: copies the selector to cmd.arena (dupeZ) and calls lp.actions.waitForSelector with timeout and bc.session; on success registers node in bc.node_registry and returns backendNodeId. Must translate action errors to appropriate CDP errors (InvalidSelector, Timeout).

key_behaviors: dispatches LP.* RPC methods to internal handlers
registers nodes in backend registry and returns backendNodeId
converts internal errors to CDP error codes

pitfalls.mistake: Registering the same node multiple times or constructing a separate node id array instead of populating backendNodeId on element objects.
pitfalls.consequence: Duplicate registrations lead to inconsistent backend node ids and confusing results for clients.
pitfalls.prevention: Always use the centralized registry.register / interactive.registerNodes helpers and let them populate backendNodeId on returned elements.

pitfalls.mistake: Returning pointers to memory allocated on cmd.arena after cmd.arena is deallocated by caller.
pitfalls.consequence: Use-after-free or corrupted output in responses.
pitfalls.prevention: Only return bytes produced via cmd.arena that remain valid until sendResult completes; allocate temporary writers with proper defer deinit.

pitfalls.mistake: Treating any internal error as a generic panic instead of mapping to CDP error codes.
pitfalls.consequence: RPC clients receive no consistent protocol error and may hang or misinterpret failures.
pitfalls.prevention: Follow established error mapping patterns: InvalidNodeType -> InvalidParam, InvalidNodeId -> InvalidNodeId, Timeout -> InternalError (or Timeout mapping where appropriate).

key_sections: getSemanticTree: constructs SemanticTree and supports text vs JSON return
getInteractiveElements: collects and registers interactive nodes
action functions (clickNode, fillNode, scrollNode): resolve backend ids and call lp.actions
skip_unless_needed: test block at end of file (useful for tests but not required for understanding handlers)

tests.example_command: zig test src/cdp/domains/lp.zig -Dtarget=x86_64-linux-gnu

relevant_snippets.description: getMarkdown test: sets up browser context, creates page and asserts result contains markdown field.
action tools test: navigates to mcp_actions.html and exercises click/fill/scroll and asserts window-scoped JS flags.

related_files.relationship: Delegates semantic tree construction and node detail extraction to this module
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to SemanticTree API (e.g. getNodeDetails signature) impact how lp domain serializes results.

related_files.relationship: Performs the actual DOM event dispatch/element operations used by clickNode, fillNode, scrollNode, waitForSelector
related_files.reason_to_check: Modifying action semantics (event ordering, return errors) requires updating error mapping and tests here.

related_files.relationship: Exposes similar tooling over MCP; shares semantics for node registration, markdown/semantic tree streaming and error mapping
related_files.reason_to_check: Protocol surfaces and node id registration must remain consistent across MCP and CDP tool implementations.

insights.title: Add LP domain method getNodeDetails
insights.problem: Need an MCP/CDP endpoint to fetch detailed node info by backendNodeId for tools/clients.
insights.root_cause: There was no RPC mapping for node details; back-end registry lookup and error handling had to be introduced.
insights.solution: Added getNodeDetails RPC handler which parses backendNodeId, looks up node via bc.node_registry, calls SemanticTree.getNodeDetails and returns nodeDetails in response.
insights.lesson_learned: Registry lookups must validate node existence and return proper MCP error codes; reuse page/registry context from cmd to ensure correct lifetime and allocator usage.

insights.title: Unify node registration for interactive elements
insights.problem: getInteractiveElements previously assembled a separate nodeIds list by directly registering nodes, duplicating logic and producing a separate nodeIds array.
insights.root_cause: Two different registration/reporting strategies (nodeIds array vs backendNodeId on elements) coexisted, causing brittle code and duplicated registrations.
insights.solution: Call interactive.registerNodes(elements, &bc.node_registry) to populate backendNodeId on each element and remove separate nodeIds array from the result.
insights.lesson_learned: Prefer augmenting the element objects themselves with backend identifiers instead of returning parallel arrays; centralize node registration to avoid inconsistencies.

src/mcp/tools.zig.short_description: MCP tools registry and handlers: tool definitions, argument parsing and tool-call implementations.
src/mcp/tools.zig.description:
This file defines the MCP 'tools' surface: a tool_list describing available tools (goto, markdown, links, evaluate, semantic_tree, click, fill, scroll, waitForSelector, hover, press, selectOption, setChecked, findElement, etc.), maps tool names to enum actions (tool_map), and implements handlers for listing and calling tools over the MCP protocol. Each tool handler parses JSON arguments, ensures a page is available (optionally navigating to a supplied URL), performs the requested operation using browser modules (lp.markdown, lp.links, lp.SemanticTree, lp.actions, lp.forms), and replies to the client using Server.sendResult or Server.sendError.

Why it exists: Provides a higher-level HTTP/JSON-friendly tool API used by agent tooling, the demo, and CLI integrations. It centralizes argument validation (parseArgs / parseArgsOrDefault), page creation/navigation (performGoto / ensurePage), node resolution (resolveNodeAndPage), and serialization/streaming of results (ToolStreamingText.jsonStringify used for streaming large text results). The design aims to provide consistent error mapping and to prevent duplicated logic across handlers.

How it fits: MCP tools are a complementary public API to CDP: they target higher-level agent use cases (tools for LLMs). The file integrates with Server, the node registry, and core browser modules. It includes convenience schemas for tool inputs (protocol.minify strings) and uses protocol types for streaming text content returns. The commit history and comments indicate careful handling of serialization failures (catch and convert to InternalError) and of navigation/wait semantics (runner.wait behavior changed historically).

key_constructs.name: tool_list
key_constructs.purpose: Declare the list of tools exposed to MCP clients with names, descriptions and JSON schemas.
key_constructs.reasoning: Authoritative list used by handleList to return capabilities to callers; modifying this changes the external API surface and must be coordinated with tool_map and handlers.

key_constructs.name: tool_map
key_constructs.purpose: Map tool names (strings) to the ToolAction enum for fast dispatch.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by handleCall to resolve the requested tool name into a compile-time enum value; keep in sync with tool_list and handler switch cases.

key_constructs.name: ToolStreamingText
key_constructs.purpose: Wrapper type for streaming large text outputs (markdown, links, semantic tree) implementing jsonStringify to lazily serialize contents.
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements custom serialization to stream content directly and avoid building whole strings in memory; jsonStringify must be robust: it runs DOM traversal and needs proper registry and arena parameters. Serialization failures are converted to protocol errors by callers.

key_constructs.name: handleCall
key_constructs.purpose: Top-level dispatcher for MCP tool invocations parsing name/arguments and dispatching to specific handlers.
key_constructs.reasoning: Parses JSON call parameters, resolves the tool action via tool_map and invokes the appropriate handler. It is central to argument validation and controlling when to call server.sendError vs sendResult.

key_constructs.name: handleMarkdown
key_constructs.purpose: Ensure a page (optionally navigating) then return streaming markdown representation as CallToolResult(TextContent).
key_constructs.reasoning: Relies on ensurePage and constructs ToolStreamingText for streaming. It wraps server.sendResult in a catch to map serialization failures to InternalError.

key_constructs.name: handleSemanticTree
key_constructs.purpose: Ensure a page and return a streaming semantic tree, possibly for a sub-node (backendNodeId) and maxDepth.
key_constructs.reasoning: Builds ToolStreamingText with registry and arena to let jsonStringify/textify the semantic tree lazily. Must ensure server.node_registry is passed correctly to allow registry.register lookups during serialization.

key_constructs.name: handleNodeDetails
key_constructs.purpose: Resolve backendNodeId to node+page and return SemanticTree.getNodeDetails as serialized JSON content.
key_constructs.reasoning: Per commit history, this was added to provide node metadata to MCP tools. It must use resolveNodeAndPage helper to map errors consistently and use json.Stringify.value on the details with proper arena lifetime.

key_constructs.name: ensurePage
key_constructs.purpose: Ensure there is a page present and optionally navigate to a supplied URL using performGoto.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes the optional navigation-before-tool pattern used by several handlers. It converts parse/nav errors to server.sendError and returns the active page on success; tests and other handlers rely on its standardized error mapping.

key_constructs.name: performGoto
key_constructs.purpose: Create page, navigate to URL, and wait per options; used by ensurePage and goto tool.
key_constructs.reasoning: Wraps navigation orchestration (page creation, runner setup, runner.wait) and must translate runner.wait and other errors to server.sendError with the historically-correct message (commit insight: changed from 'Timeout waiting for page load' to 'Error waiting for page load').

key_constructs.name: resolveNodeAndPage
key_constructs.purpose: Map backend node id to node pointer and current page, returning both for handlers that operate on nodes.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizing resolution ensures consistent error messages for missing nodes or missing pages and reduces duplication across click/fill/select/press handlers.

key_behaviors: registers and exposes tools and their schemas to MCP clients
parses and validates JSON tool arguments
navigates and ensures page state before tool execution
streams large text results and maps serialization errors to protocol errors

pitfalls.mistake: Altering tool_list entries without updating tool_map or switch-case dispatch.
pitfalls.consequence: Tool names in the list can become unsynchronized with the runtime dispatch, causing MethodNotFound or wrong handler being invoked.
pitfalls.prevention: When editing tool_list, ensure tool_map and the handleCall switch include matching names and enum entries.

pitfalls.mistake: Allowing json serialization to fail without catching and sending server.sendError.
pitfalls.consequence: Caller sees no error or the process may panic; serialization failures must be translated to InternalError responses.
pitfalls.prevention: Wrap server.sendResult calls for streaming or text content in catch blocks and call server.sendError(id, .InternalError, "Failed to serialize ...").

pitfalls.mistake: Changing ensurePage/performGoto semantics (timeouts, runner.wait return values) without updating error messages mapping.
pitfalls.consequence: Clients historically expect certain error strings; tests and integration rely on consistent error mapping. Mis-mapped errors can break client logic that checks for specific messages.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the error mapping policy described in commit history (e.g., use 'Error waiting for page load' rather than an older 'Timeout waiting for page load').

pitfalls.mistake: Streaming ToolStreamingText that references server.node_registry or arena that becomes invalid before jsonStringify runs.
pitfalls.consequence: Use-after-free or invalid registry lookups during serialization.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure the registry reference (server.node_registry) and arena live at least as long as serialization; do not hand out ToolStreamingText with ephemeral pointers.

key_sections: tool_list / tool_map: authoritative tool surface and dispatch mapping
parseArgs / parseArgsOrDefault: argument parsing helpers and error behavior
ensurePage / performGoto: navigation and page-prep patterns
ToolStreamingText.jsonStringify: streaming serialization logic for large text outputs
skip_unless_needed: protocol.minify long inline JSON schemas (readable but can be skipped when focusing on behavior)

exercised_by: embedded tests in tools.zig (project-wide Zig tests)
test_functions: MCP - Actions: click, fill, scroll, hover, press, selectOption, setChecked
tests.example_command: zig test src/mcp/tools.zig -Dtarget=x86_64-linux-gnu

relevant_snippets.description: DOM fixture used by MCP actions tests to assert DOM-level side-effects such as window.clicked and window.inputVal.

related_files.relationship: CDP domain exposes similar features for external clients; both must stay consistent in node registration and action semantics
related_files.reason_to_check: If you change action semantics, error mappings, or node registry behavior in one, update the other to keep consistent API surface.

related_files.relationship: Implements low-level DOM event dispatch and state mutation used by many tools (click, fill, hover, selectOption, setChecked, press, scroll)
related_files.reason_to_check: Tool handlers call these functions directly and rely on their error returns and event ordering.

related_files.relationship: Provides high-level types (Page, Session) and modules the tools call; used to create Browser/Session/Page instances
related_files.reason_to_check: Changing Page/Session lifecycle will affect ensurePage/performGoto and tool behavior.

data_constants_to_check: protocol schemas used in tool_list must remain valid JSON
ensure timeouts/defaults align with lp.Config.WaitUntil and runner.wait expectations

insights.title: Report generic error waiting for page load instead of 'Timeout waiting for page load'
insights.problem: When Runner wait returned done, tools previously treated it as a Timeout and sent a specific 'Timeout waiting for page load' message.
insights.root_cause: Change in Runner semantics required tools to map .done to a more general error string.
insights.solution: Adjust error message to 'Error waiting for page load' to be accurate for the new semantics.

insights.title: Consolidate tool schemas and add findElement tool
insights.problem: Tool schemas were duplicated inline and some tools lacked useful helpers for agents.
insights.root_cause: Schema strings repeated across many tool entries and the repository had no simple 'findElement' MCP tool.
insights.solution: Introduce reusable schema constants, add findElement tool (role/name matching), and centralize argument parsing (commits 58fc60d, 46a63e0, 1854627).
insights.lesson_learned: When a protocol surface grows, extract common schema pieces and helper functions to reduce duplicate maintenance and to make new tools consistent.

insights.title: Centralize node+page resolution
insights.problem: Multiple handlers repeated logic to check session.currentPage and node_registry lookup, causing duplication and inconsistent error handling.
insights.root_cause: Each tool handler duplicated the same precondition checks and error responses.
insights.solution: Use resolveNodeAndPage helper (moved into src/mcp/tools.zig by commit 6c9a5dd) to return unified node & page resolving and consistent error reporting.
insights.lesson_learned: Centralize validation and lookup logic for consistent error messages and to reduce future bugs caused by slight divergences between handlers.

insights.title: Add MCP tests for actions and findElement
insights.problem: Integration of MCP actions lacked comprehensive tests covering hover, press, selectOption, setChecked and findElement scenarios.
insights.root_cause: New action tools were added without end-to-end test coverage for all relevant interactions.
insights.solution: Added tests inside src/mcp/tools.zig and new mcp_actions.html fixtures exercising the tools' responses (commits 5e79af4, 58143ee, 46a63e0).
insights.lesson_learned: When adding platform behaviors (user events), include tests validating both tool RPC results and page-observed side-effects.
constructs: test "MCP - Actions: click, fill, scroll, hover, press, selectOption, setChecked"

insights.title: CallToolResult simplified to text-only
insights.problem: Protocol previously carried both image and text tool results, but screenshot was removed/changed.
insights.root_cause: Screenshot tool was removed and protocol contained unused ImageContent type.
insights.solution: Removed ImageContent and simplified CallToolResult to only TextContent; updated all callers accordingly (commit 1854627).
insights.lesson_learned: Protocol changes must be applied consistently across tool implementations; prefer explicit schema constants to make such migrations safer.

insights.title: Improve navigation reliability & CDP support
insights.problem: MCP navigation used hard-coded/short timeout and lacked options for wait strategies and CDP server co-running.
insights.root_cause: No per-tool timeout/waitUntil configuration; CDP server integration and error handling incomplete.
insights.solution: Added timeout and waitUntil args, increased default navigation timeout, added navigate/eval/screenshot tool entries, and added support for running a CDP server alongside MCP (commit 58fc60d). Later screenshot removal and schema cleanup occurred in other commits.
insights.lesson_learned: High-level agent APIs must expose timeouts and wait strategies; also ensure tools can run alongside optional subsystems like CDP.

insights.title: Expose nodeDetails tool to MCP tools list and handler
insights.problem: Tooling clients needed a CLI/HTTP tool to request node metadata from the server.
insights.root_cause: Tools list and handler switch didn't include nodeDetails.
insights.solution: Added tool definition to tool_list, mapped new ToolAction.nodeDetails, added handler handleNodeDetails which validates params, gets node from registry and calls SemanticTree.getNodeDetails, serializes JSON result and returns as CallToolResult.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding a tool endpoint, provide inputSchema, map action enums, and reuse existing registry/session helpers to validate page/node presence. Use server.session.currentPage() for page validation and uniform error mapping.

insights.title: Fail MCP call with error when argument parsing fails
insights.problem: Tools previously returned default structs on invalid arguments silently; callers expected explicit error responses.
insights.root_cause: parseArgsOrDefault swallowed JSON parse errors and returned default value, making it hard for clients to know their request was invalid.
insights.solution: Change parseArgsOrDefault signature to accept server and id and send server.sendError(id, .InvalidParams, "Invalid arguments") on parse failure, returning error.InvalidParams. Update all tool handlers to use try parseArgsOrDefault so error propagates and an error response is sent to client.
insights.lesson_learned: RPC handlers should validate arguments and reply with protocol errors on invalid input rather than silently using defaults — this avoids confusing client behavior and aids debugging.

insights.title: Return MCP internal error on serialization failures for tools
insights.problem: Tool handlers used try server.sendResult(...) and callers could panic or lose context when result serialization failed.
insights.root_cause: No centralized mapping of serialization failure to MCP error response.
insights.solution: Wrap server.sendResult in catch and call server.sendError(id, .InternalError, "Failed to serialize ...") on failure for markdown/links/semantic-tree handlers.
insights.lesson_learned: Always translate internal serialization errors into protocol-level errors so callers observe the failure explicitly.

insights.title: Extract parseOptionalAndGetPage pattern into helpers
insights.problem: Six MCP tool handlers duplicated code to optionally parse a URL param, navigate to it, and then fetch current page — leading to repeated code and inconsistent error handling.
insights.root_cause: Handlers in tools.zig each implemented the same URL-parsing/navigation/get-page pattern inline.
insights.solution: Introduced UrlParams, parseArgsOrDefault(), parseArgs(), ensurePage() helpers and replaced inline code in handlers (markdown, links, semantic_tree, interactiveElements, structuredData, detectForms) with calls to these helpers. Centralized navigation and consistent error reporting.
insights.lesson_learned: Consolidate repeated request parsing and page-creation logic into well-named helpers that encapsulate error handling, navigation side-effects, and MCP error responses.

insights.title: Make page creation and runner startup report MCP errors instead of panicking
insights.problem: Page creation and runner startup could fail and errors were not turned into MCP-friendly error messages.
insights.root_cause: Earlier code sometimes used try without translating failures into server.sendError responses, and ignored runner.wait errors.
insights.solution: performGoto now sends server.sendError for page creation or runner start failures and returns error.NavigationFailed. runner.wait errors also send an InternalError and return. ensurePage uses server.sendError to notify the client when no page is available.
insights.lesson_learned: When code is part of an RPC handler, convert internal failures into protocol error responses and avoid panics; consistently report timeouts and start failures.

insights.title: Use central link collector and register nodes before serializing
insights.problem: Tools serialized links and interactive elements with duplicated traversal and node registration logic.
insights.root_cause: Link collection and registration were implemented inline in streaming and MCP handlers.
insights.solution: Call lp.links.collectLinks for the links tool and lp.interactive.registerNodes to populate backendNodeId before JSONifying interactive elements. Improved error logging when link query fails.
insights.lesson_learned: Keep DOM traversal/utilities in browser modules and call registry registration centrally before serializing results to ensure consistent output for clients.

src/browser/tests/mcp_actions.html.short_description: HTML fixture page with elements and listeners to exercise MCP action tools in tests.
src/browser/tests/mcp_actions.html.description:
This HTML file is a lightweight DOM fixture used by MCP and CDP tests to validate action semantics. It contains a button, inputs, select elements, a scrollable box and additional inputs for hover/keyboard/select/checkbox/radio tests. Each element sets flags on the window (e.g., window.clicked, window.inputVal, window.changed) via inline handlers or added event listeners so test code can evaluate JavaScript in the page context to assert the side-effects of lp.actions or MCP tools.

Why it exists: Tests rely on easily observable side-effects that do not require complex server-side glue; by reading window-scoped variables after invoking actions, tests can confirm both the firing of events and the correct value propagation (e.g., select option value, checkbox change). The fixture intentionally uses ids (btn, inp, sel, scrollbox, hoverTarget, keyTarget, sel2, chk, rad) that test code references directly.

How it fits: This page is fetched by tests (see src/cdp/domains/lp.zig tests) via a local test HTTP server (started by the Zig test harness), navigated to by Page.navigate, and then the test runner waits shortly for scripts to set up and then triggers MCP/CDP actions. After actions run, the test harness executes a JS expression in the page to validate that the expected window variables are set.

key_constructs.name: btn
key_constructs.purpose: Button element with id 'btn' used to test click action and its onclick handler sets window.clicked = true.
key_constructs.reasoning: Test code registers the node and invokes click via tools; the onclick handler provides a simple boolean side-effect to assert click success.

key_constructs.name: inp
key_constructs.purpose: Input element with id 'inp' used to test fill action; oninput sets window.inputVal and onchange sets window.changed = true.
key_constructs.reasoning: Separation of oninput and onchange lets tests verify that both input value and change event are dispatched in correct order.

key_constructs.name: sel
key_constructs.purpose: Select element with id 'sel' used to test filling/selecting options and change event propagation.
key_constructs.reasoning: Changing selection and verifying window.selChanged demonstrates selectOption and fill behavior.

key_behaviors: provides elements that set window-scoped flags on user events
used by tests to assert results of click/fill/scroll/hover/press/select/setChecked actions

pitfalls.mistake: Changing element ids or removing inline listeners
pitfalls.consequence: Tests that rely on those ids/listeners will break (they depend on window.* flags being set).
pitfalls.prevention: Keep ids and event listeners stable or update tests consistently when changing the fixture.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering inline JS to use async logic without updating test wait timings
pitfalls.consequence: Tests may flakily pass or fail due to timing; tests assume handlers run synchronously or are observable after a short runner.wait.
pitfalls.prevention: If introducing async behaviors, also update test runner wait durations or use explicit waitForSelector/waitForScript integration.

reading_guide.start_here: body element — scan element ids: btn, inp, sel, scrollbox for targets referenced by tests
key_sections: button#btn onclick: window.clicked flag
input#inp oninput/onchange: window.inputVal & window.changed
select#sel onchange: window.selChanged
div#scrollbox onscroll: window.scrolled
skip_unless_needed: hoverTarget, keyTarget, sel2, chk, rad elements for additional action tests

test_functions: MCP - Actions: click, fill, scroll, hover, press, selectOption, setChecked
tests.example_command: Start local test HTTP server (zig test harness) and run zig test for the corresponding Zig test file (see repo Makefile or zig build test).

relevant_snippets.description: Primary elements used by action tests (button, input, select, scrollbox).

related_files.relationship: Test harness in lp.zig navigates to this HTML fixture to exercise action handlers
related_files.reason_to_check: If tests change expected flags or element ids, update this fixture.

related_files.relationship: MCP tools' tests use the same fixture for integration verification of tool actions
related_files.reason_to_check: If action semantics change (e.g., event ordering), both tests and fixture may need adjustments.

insights.title: Add hover/key/select/checkbox/radio test elements and listeners
insights.problem: No DOM elements existed to exercise new hover/press/select/setChecked behavior.
insights.root_cause: New action tools required matching DOM fixtures for end-to-end assertions.
insights.solution: Added elements and attached listeners to assert side-effects from MCP actions (commit 58143ee and 5e79af4).
insights.lesson_learned: When adding tools that interact with the DOM, update test pages so behavior can be observed via JS assertions.

src/lightpanda.zig.short_description: Top-level Lightpanda runtime exports and helper utilities (fetch flow, assertions, RC helper).
src/lightpanda.zig.description:
This file is the central, top-level Zig module that re-exports and wires many of the project's public runtime components and provides a handful of cross-cutting helpers. It exists to present a single importable namespace (lp/lightpanda) that other modules and the MCP/CDP layers import. It defines the public aliases for App, Browser, Page, HTTP client, CDP nodes, dump/markdown tooling, and low-level utilities (assert, RC) so consumers can import `@import("lightpanda")` and access the main runtime surface quickly.

Beyond re-exports, this file implements an application-level helper function fetch(...) used by CLI commands and tools to perform a single navigation lifecycle: initialize HttpClient and Notification, create Browser/Session/Page, navigate to a URL, wait according to several strategies, and optionally write a dump in various formats. It also contains the dumpWPT helper for extracting Web Platform Test (WPT) report details by executing JS in the page. Two other important responsibilities are the custom assertion helper (assert + assertionFailure) which funnels assertion failures into the project's crash handler and a generic compile-time RC(comptime T: type) meta-type used for simple reference-counted objects which call `deinit(session)` once reference count reaches zero.

Design decisions visible here: the fetch helper uses explicit resource init/deinit pairs with Zig defers to scope-manage lifetimes; it uses `page.call_arena` for temporary allocations when interacting with URL encoding and JS evaluation; it centralizes dump format handling (html, markdown, semantic_tree, wpt) in one place so CLI and tools produce consistent outputs; the RC meta-type is a lightweight generic to manage reference counting with an explicit release path that calls deinit on the wrapped value. The assert helper checks a compile-time IS_DEBUG flag and funnels failures through the crash handler when not in compile-time or when runtime assertions must abort, preserving consistent crash semantics across build modes.

key_constructs.name: FetchOpts
key_constructs.purpose: Holds named options controlling fetch() behavior (timeouts, wait strategies, dump format, writer).
key_constructs.reasoning: Modifying fetch behavior or wiring new CLI flags requires updating this struct; its defaults (wait_ms=5000) are used if callers omit values.

key_constructs.name: fetch
key_constructs.purpose: Performs a one-shot navigation: initialize HTTP/browser, navigate, wait, and write dumps to a writer if provided.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is used by CLI tooling and tools exposed via MCP; changes here affect navigation lifecycle, waiting semantics, and all dump outputs invoked from tools/CLI.

key_constructs.name: dumpWPT
key_constructs.purpose: Runs a JS snippet in the page to extract WPT report data and writes a JSON summary to the provided writer.
key_constructs.reasoning: Interacts with the JS VM (page.js.localScope and TryCatch). Maintains arena lifetimes and handles JS exceptions specially — edits must keep the try/catch and arena allocation logic intact.

key_constructs.name: assert
key_constructs.purpose: Project-level assertion helper that either traps in Debug mode or routes to assertionFailure in other modes.
key_constructs.reasoning: This function encodes how assertions behave across build modes. The IS_DEBUG branch and usage of unreachable vs. assertionFailure has changed across commits, so preserving expected debug vs release semantics is critical for consistent failure/reporting behavior.

key_constructs.name: assertionFailure
key_constructs.purpose: Runtime assertion failure path that calls crash_handler.crash with context and return address (noreturn).
key_constructs.reasoning: This function provides the canonical crash path and is wired to the project's crash telemetry and reporting hooks. Any change affects global crash behavior and the format/context of crash reports.

key_constructs.name: RC
key_constructs.purpose: Comptime generic that returns a small reference-counted struct type with init/acquire/release/format methods.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used as a lightweight reference counting primitive. release(...) asserts the invariant `_refs > 0` and calls value.deinit(session) when count hits zero; calling or changing release semantics affects object finalization across the codebase.

key_constructs.name: IS_DEBUG
key_constructs.purpose: Comptime boolean reflecting Zig build mode .Debug used by assert to decide behavior.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by assert to decide whether to call unreachable at compile-time; changes alter debug-time traps vs. runtime crash reporting.

key_constructs.name: HttpClient
key_constructs.purpose: Alias to the project's browser/HttpClient.zig module used to initialize network clients in fetch().
key_constructs.reasoning: fetch() depends on HttpClient.init/deinit contract; any changes in HttpClient API must be reconciled with calls here.

key_behaviors: initializes HttpClient, Notification, Browser and Session for a one-shot navigation
navigates to an encoded URL and waits using wait_until / wait_selector / wait_script rules
serializes page output into multiple dump formats (html, markdown, semantic_tree, wpt)
provides project-wide assertion and fatal-crash routing
provides a generic RC meta-type for reference-counted objects which call deinit(session)

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying RC.release to avoid asserting _refs > 0 or removing the type context from the assertion.
pitfalls.consequence: Underflow of the reference counter can occur silently in non-debug builds, hiding the source logic bug and leading to double-deinit or memory corruption.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the unconditional assert(_refs > 0, ...) check and the inclusion of type context in the message; do not make it debug-only.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing assertion behavior (assert/unreachable/assertionFailure) without understanding debug vs release expectations.
