pitfalls.consequence: Debug builds may stop at different points (trap vs crash handler), which changes developer debugging experience and may affect tests that expect a specific failure mechanism.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the IS_DEBUG combinator and the historical toggling behavior noted in commit history; keep crash_handler pathway intact for non-debug builds.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering fetch()'s wait timing calculations or arena lifetimes (e.g., removing page.call_arena usage).
pitfalls.consequence: Incorrect remaining-time calculation, under/overshooting timeouts, or use-after-free when JS-to-Zig temporary allocations outlive the arena.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the timer -> elapsed conversion and remaining calculation semantics; ensure allocations for URL encoding and JS-to-string use the page.call_arena as before.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing the try/catch pattern in dumpWPT or changing how JS errors are captured and reported.
pitfalls.consequence: JS exceptions could propagate as Zig errors or heap-allocated strings without proper arena-managed lifetime, creating crashes or memory leaks.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep js.TryCatch usage and the caughtOrError conversion path; preserve writer error path that prints the caught JS error.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming opts.writer is always present and removing the early return `const writer = opts.writer orelse return;`
pitfalls.consequence: Code paths that assume a writer will exist could dereference null and crash; fetch() is intended to be usable without producing a dump (writer optional).
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the optional writer semantics: if writer is null, fetch completes without writing output.

key_sections: fetch: the navigation lifecycle and dump-mode switch (core runtime behavior called by CLI/tools)
dumpWPT: how WPT results are extracted from the page JS context (JS TryCatch and arena usage)
RC: the reference-counting meta-type and the semantics of release(...) (finalization contract)
assert + assertionFailure: project-wide crash/assert semantics and crash_handler integration
skip_unless_needed: the long list of re-export declarations at the top (boilerplate aliases)
commented-out CPU/heap profiler blocks unless profiling interaction is required

test_functions: refAllDecls (invoked via std.testing.refAllDecls in the file's test block)
tests.example_command: zig test src/lightpanda.zig

relevant_snippets.description: file-local Zig test block calling std.testing.refAllDecls(@This()) ensures public symbols are referenceable for the test harness

related_files.relationship: Provides MCP/CDP domain implementations that call into the exports and datastructures re-exported by lightpanda.zig (semantic tree, markdown, interactive, structured_data).
related_files.reason_to_check: If fetch/dump format or SemanticTree/Node re-exports change here, the CDP handlers must be updated to match the new API surface.

related_files.relationship: Defines remote 'tools' that map JSON tool invocations to functions such as navigation, markdown and evaluate which use fetch() and related exports in this file.
related_files.reason_to_check: Tool schemas and tooling names refer to dump/markdown/evaluate behaviors; changes to FetchOpts or fetch semantics can require protocol/schema updates here.

related_files.relationship: HttpClient init/deinit are called from fetch(); this file expects the HttpClient API's init signature and lifetime behavior.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any API changes to HttpClient.init/deinit must be reconciled with the callsite in fetch().

related_files.relationship: fetch() relies on Page.navigate, page.call_arena and page.window._document; RC.release calls value.deinit(session) which many page-level types implement.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to Page lifecycle, runner.wait APIs, or deinit(session) signatures affect fetch() and RC semantics.

insights.title: Add assertion on reference-count release overflow
insights.problem: Release() could be called when _refs == 0, which would underflow and hide the real bug.
insights.root_cause: In release() there was only a debug-only assert; in non-debug builds an underflow could occur silently.
insights.solution: Always assert self._refs > 0 with context message (type name) to catch release overflow even in non-debug builds.
insights.lesson_learned: Reference counting invariants should be enforced in all build modes (or at least fail loudly) to catch logic errors early; include type context to help debugging.

insights.title: Remove finalizer_callbacks automatic cleanup in RC.release
insights.problem: RC deinit used to remove finalizer_callbacks entry as part of release path which mingled RC release with finalizer bookkeeping.
insights.root_cause: Finalizer callbacks were previously used to hide missing releaseRefs; RC deinit assumed finalizer_callbacks contained an entry to free arena.
insights.solution: Stop removing finalizer callbacks from RC.release path; finalizer callback management moved to Session semantics (commit 77b60ce).
insights.lesson_learned: Keep low-level RC mechanics isolated from VM finalizer bookkeeping to avoid cross-responsibility coupling; finalizer fallback logic belongs in VM/session layer.

insights.title: RC.format added for debug printing
insights.problem: There was no small helper to format reference-counted objects for diagnostics.
insights.root_cause: Lack of convenient formatting for debug/logging information about reference counts.
insights.solution: Add pub fn format(self: @This(), writer: *std.Io.Writer) to RC meta-type to print the reference count (commit 492fd86).
insights.lesson_learned: Add small formatting helpers for core meta-types used heavily in debugging to simplify diagnostics and logs.

insights.title: Expose new links module
insights.problem: New link-collection utilities were added but not exported for usage by MCP tooling.
insights.root_cause: New module needed to be imported by other modules.
insights.solution: Added 'links' import alias to top-level lightpanda.zig.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding a shared module, ensure it's re-exported/imported where needed to avoid missing references.

insights.title: Toggle unreachable() call inside assert helper across commits
insights.problem: Different commits alternately enabled/disabled an unreachable() in assert's debug branch which affects whether debug builds hit unreachable or call assertionFailure.
insights.root_cause: Minor changes to how assertions are expressed (developer preference) toggled whether unreachable is called in debug builds.
insights.solution: Code was switched back and forth; ensure consistent expected behavior for debug builds (either use unreachable() to trigger debug trap or rely on assertionFailure).
insights.lesson_learned: Be deliberate about debug-only unreachable traps versus controlled assertion failures; changing this midstream can affect debugging behavior and test outcomes.

src/SemanticTree.zig.short_description: Builds and serializes an accessibility-like semantic tree from DOM Nodes (JSON/text and node details).
src/SemanticTree.zig.description:
This module walks a DOM subtree and produces higher-level semantic information consumed by CDP endpoints and tooling. It exists to centralize logic that: (1) classifies nodes by accessibility role (AXNode helpers), (2) computes node names and values, (3) decides which nodes should be visited or pruned (prune vs interactive_only modes), (4) registers nodes in the CDP node registry (producing backend node ids), and (5) serializes the result as either JSON (std.json.Stringify) or a compact text dump. The file also exposes helpers used by the lp CDP domain (getNodeDetails) to produce richer per-node metadata for RPCs.

Design decisions visible in the file include a visitor pattern (JsonVisitor and TextVisitor) decoupling traversal from serialization; use of a registry to guarantee stable backend node ids; incremental XPath construction using an ArrayList that is shrunk back to previous length after visiting children; and explicit caches passed through the walk (visibility_cache and pointer_events_cache) to avoid repeated expensive computations. The walker intentionally filters nodes early (metadata, svg, datalist/option/optgroup) and respects visibility and HTML hidden attributes. There is a subtle pruning policy informed by AX roles and whether nodes are interactive or have explicit labels to avoid token bloat and incorrectly hoisting text nodes.

This module interacts closely with: browser/interactive.zig (listener map and interactivity classification), cdp/AXNode.zig (AXNode.getRole/getName helpers), cdp/Node.zig (registry and backend id), and browser/webapi Node/Element/CData types (the DOM runtime). It is used by the CDP lp domain implementation (src/cdp/domains/lp.zig) for the getSemanticTree and getNodeDetails RPCs. Changes here must preserve the exact semantics of visitor lifecycle and registry side effects because other parts of the system (CDP RPCs and node registries) depend on stable node ids, correct XPath strings, and whether children are walked or unrolled when nodes are skipped.

key_constructs.name: jsonStringify
key_constructs.purpose: Entry point that serializes the semantic tree as JSON via std.json.Stringify and a JsonVisitor.
key_constructs.reasoning: Preserves ordering of beginObject/endObject calls and builds listener/visibility caches once per dump; must be called with a valid allocator and page context to build listener map used by interactivity classification.

key_constructs.name: textStringify
key_constructs.purpose: Entry point that produces a human-readable text dump of the semantic tree using TextVisitor.
key_constructs.reasoning: Uses the same traversal/caches as jsonStringify; outputs formatting and indentation controlled by depth, must keep writer semantics intact.

key_constructs.name: walk
key_constructs.purpose: Recursive DOM walker that applies pruning/interactivity rules, registers nodes, computes AX role/name/value/options, builds XPath, and invokes visitors.
key_constructs.reasoning: Core traversal logic: controls whether a visitor.visit() is called, whether children are unrolled, and that visitor.leave() is only invoked if visit() was executed. Any edits must keep the invariants around did_visit/should_walk_children to avoid mismatched JSON/text structure or missing leave() calls.

key_constructs.name: OptionData
key_constructs.purpose: Small struct representing an option's value, text and selected state for select/datalist elements.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to export select/datalist options in both JSON and NodeDetails; allocation is arena-based via ArrayList -> toOwnedSlice.

key_constructs.name: NodeData
key_constructs.purpose: Transient per-node data collected by walk() and passed to visitors (contains id, AXNode, role, name, value, options, checked, xpath, interactive, disabled, tag_name).
key_constructs.reasoning: Represents the semantic view of a Node. The JSON/text visitors, and other helpers expect these exact fields: e.g., 'checked' was recently added and must be preserved when serializing inputs.

key_constructs.name: WalkContext
key_constructs.purpose: Context passed through recursion that contains the XPath buffer and cached resources (listener targets, visibility and pointer event caches).
key_constructs.reasoning: Avoids recomputing expensive maps/caches per node and centralizes ownership of the XPath buffer used to build concise XPath strings.

key_constructs.name: extractSelectOptions
key_constructs.purpose: Collects option elements from a select or optgroup subtree and returns an arena-allocated slice of OptionData.
key_constructs.reasoning: Called for <select> elements; it uses page helpers (Option.Html.Option methods) and allocator semantics; must return owned memory tied to the provided allocator.

key_constructs.name: extractDataListOptions
key_constructs.purpose: Resolves a datalist reference by id on the page and delegates to extractSelectOptions to gather option items.
key_constructs.reasoning: Returns null if the referenced element isn't a datalist or not found; callers expect a ?[]OptionData return value.

key_constructs.name: appendXPathSegment
key_constructs.purpose: Appends a single XPath segment for the current node to the provided writer (either element tag or text()).
key_constructs.reasoning: The exact formatting (/tag[index] or /text()[index]) is relied upon by downstream consumers; any change will affect XPath strings returned by RPCs.

key_constructs.name: JsonVisitor
key_constructs.purpose: Visitor that serializes a visited node into a JSON object, writes children array start and returns whether children should be walked.
key_constructs.reasoning: Must maintain JSON structure: beginObject/fields/children array/endObject. It also signals when options are present to prevent walking children (children handled natively).

key_constructs.name: TextVisitor
key_constructs.purpose: Visitor that writes a textual representation of a node including id, role, name, value, checked and interactivity markers.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used for the text format of getSemanticTree; formatting and the conditions under which fields are printed (e.g. only non-empty values, disabled marker) are part of the tool output contract.

key_constructs.name: isStructuralRole
key_constructs.purpose: Checks whether a role string matches a predefined set of structural roles that are candidates for pruning.
key_constructs.reasoning: Pruning decisions depend on this; the set of roles (e.g. 'none', 'generic', 'list', 'table', 'cell', etc.) is hard-coded and semantic; changing it will change which nodes are pruned.

key_constructs.name: getNodeDetails
key_constructs.purpose: Helper that collects a rich, single-node NodeDetails struct used by the CDP 'getNodeDetails' RPC (collects attributes, state, value, input type, options, checked, etc.).
key_constructs.reasoning: This function is called by src/cdp/domains/lp.zig and must keep its allocator-first parameter order (commit history indicates this API was reordered). Callers expect a stable shape for NodeDetails and allocator semantics for returned strings/slices.

key_constructs.name: NodeDetails
key_constructs.purpose: A struct returned by getNodeDetails containing authoritative per-node metadata (id, attributes like href/placeholder/class, isInteractive, isDisabled, checked, value, input type, options, etc.).
key_constructs.reasoning: Acts as the canonical payload for single-node inspection endpoints; other code may pattern-match on these fields. The commit history shows NodeDetails was added to centralize metadata for RPCs.

key_behaviors: serializes a DOM subtree to JSON for semanticTree RPC
produces a compact textual semantic dump
collects and returns detailed per-node metadata for inspection
registers nodes with CDP registry to generate backend node ids
prunes or filters nodes based on accessibility role, interactivity, and explicit labels

pitfalls.mistake: Change the order or types of getNodeDetails parameters (especially allocator placement) without updating all call sites.
pitfalls.consequence: Call sites (e.g., src/cdp/domains/lp.zig) will fail to compile or call with wrong allocator causing memory mismanagement.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the allocator-first convention. If altering signature, update all callers (search for SemanticTree.getNodeDetails).

pitfalls.mistake: Modify visitor lifecycle so that visit() and leave() calls become unbalanced (e.g., call leave() when visit() was skipped).
pitfalls.consequence: JSON/Text output will be malformed; std.json.Stringify calls may be mismatched (begin/end) causing runtime errors or invalid output.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the did_visit/should_walk_children separation intact: only call visitor.leave() when visitor.visit() executed.

pitfalls.mistake: Remove or change XPath formatting in appendXPathSegment.
pitfalls.consequence: Downstream consumers and RPCs relying on XPath strings will get incompatible XPath values; tests and demos may fail.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve exact formatting (/tag[index] and /text()[index]) or coordinate updates across all consumers.

pitfalls.mistake: Ignore visibility and hidden checks before registering nodes or serializing.
pitfalls.consequence: Hidden or display:none nodes may be exposed in semantic output incorrectly and cause unexpected behavior in clients.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect el.checkVisibilityCached(ctx.visibility_cache, page) and HTML hidden attributes; use provided visibility/pointer caches.

pitfalls.mistake: Alter the structural role list or pruning logic without considering interactivity and explicit labels.
pitfalls.consequence: Important text may be pruned or retained incorrectly, leading to token bloat or missing StaticText nodes in outputs.
pitfalls.prevention: When editing pruning rules, validate outputs against representative pages and run integration tests that exercise getSemanticTree/getInteractiveElements.

key_sections: JsonVisitor: how JSON output is structured and when children are walked
TextVisitor: textual formatting rules and which fields are printed
getNodeDetails: single-node inspection contract and allocator semantics
prune / interactive_only logic near walk(): to understand which nodes are skipped
skip_unless_needed: isStructuralRole static role map (only change if you intend to modify pruning behavior)
low-level option extraction loops unless working with select/datalist handling

tests.example_command: zig build test (or make test) to run the project's test suite which may exercise this module via CDP domain tests

related_files.relationship: Consumer: calls SemanticTree.getNodeDetails and constructs a SemanticTree instance for getSemanticTree/getMarkdown/getInteractiveElements RPCs.
related_files.reason_to_check: If you change public function signatures or NodeDetails shape, update the CDP RPC serialization and its parameter parsing accordingly.

related_files.relationship: Dependency: provides listener target map, classifyInteractivity and helpers used to decide interactivity for nodes.
related_files.reason_to_check: Pruning and interactive_only decisions depend on classifyInteractivity and the listener map; changing either side affects which nodes are considered interactive.

related_files.relationship: Dependency: AXNode.getRole/getName are called to compute roles and names; they shape what is exposed in the semantic output.
related_files.reason_to_check: If AXNode role/name behaviour changes, SemanticTree output semantics will change; validate both modules together.

related_files.relationship: Dependency: the registry and CDPNode ids are produced and used in NodeData.id/backendDOMNodeId fields emitted by visitors.
related_files.reason_to_check: Modifying registration or id semantics requires ensuring emitted ids remain stable for clients and node_registry lookup.

insights.title: Reorder getNodeDetails parameter order for allocator first
insights.problem: getNodeDetails had allocator as the last parameter which made some call-sites inconvenient; a refactor reorders parameters (commit 0082352).
insights.root_cause: Callsites prefer allocator-first ordering (consistent with other APIs).
insights.solution: Change signature to getNodeDetails(arena, node, registry, page) and update callers.
insights.lesson_learned: Keep allocator/arena parameters first to match repository-wide conventions and reduce accidental misuse.

insights.title: Add 'checked' state and rename fields for clarity
insights.problem: SemanticTree lacked explicit 'checked' information for input elements, and field names (is_interactive/is_disabled/node_name) were inconsistent or less clear.
insights.root_cause: Missing detail (checked) and inconsistent naming conventions made downstream consumers do extra work/guessing.
insights.solution: Add optional checked: ?bool to NodeData and include it in output; rename is_interactive->interactive, is_disabled->disabled, node_name->tag_name and update visitors and text output. Update getNodeDetails to populate checked for checkboxes/radios and adapt callers (commit fc057a3).
insights.lesson_learned: Evolve semantic models to include packed state (checked) when it is relevant for tooling. Prefer concise, domain-specific field names (interactive/disabled/tag_name) to match consumer expectations.

insights.title: Add NodeDetails tool and track is_disabled on semantic nodes
insights.problem: Tooling needed richer node metadata (role, name, interactivity, disabled state, attributes, options) for inspection/automation.
insights.root_cause: Semantic tree previously only tracked a subset of accessibility/element metadata (no disabled state, no node details extraction helper), making it cumbersome to expose a single RPC returning full node information.
insights.solution: Added NodeDetails struct and getNodeDetails function which collects attributes (id, class, href, placeholder), state (isInteractive, isDisabled, checked), value, input type and select/datalist options. Also extended NodeData to include is_disabled and updated JSON/text visitors to emit it.
insights.lesson_learned: When exposing inspection tools, centralize metadata extraction in a single helper and ensure semantic tree tracks UI states (disabled) that affect behavior. Reuse existing AXNode helpers (getRole/getName) and interactive classification code to produce consistent results.

insights.title: Include disabled state in semantic node output
insights.problem: Disabled elements were not surfaced in the semantic tree output, potentially misleading consumers about interactivity.
insights.root_cause: is_disabled state wasn't tracked in NodeData and not emitted in JSON/text visitors.
insights.solution: Added is_disabled to NodeData, populated it during walk() with el.isDisabled(), and emitted it in JsonVisitor/TextVisitor.
insights.lesson_learned: UI state flags (disabled) must be explicitly tracked and serialized; adding a single boolean to node model and ensuring all serializers respect it prevents inconsistent client behavior.

src/browser/forms.zig.short_description: Collects form structures from a DOM root, serializes them to JSON and provides tests for form edge cases
src/browser/forms.zig.description:
This module implements form discovery and representation for the headless browser runtime. It walks a DOM subtree, finds <form> elements and associated form-associated elements (including elements referencing a form via form="id"), and constructs lightweight FormInfo/FormField/SelectOption structures that capture tag, name, type, required/disabled flags, values, placeholders and select options. It exists to provide a stable, serializable view of forms for higher-level features like the MCP/CDP 'detectForms' tool and node detail extraction.

Design choices: the implementation uses a TreeWalker to traverse the DOM and relies on Element.getElements() (Form.getElements()) to collect form-associated elements — this ensures behavior consistent with browsers for external form controls. Memory for values/strings and arrays is provided by a caller-supplied arena allocator; the returned slices borrow memory from that arena (documented in the function comment). The module separates concerns into small functions: collectForms (public entry), collectFormFields (per-form field extraction), collectSelectOptions (collect options for a select), and registerNodes (associate Node pointers with backend ids via a registry abstraction). JSON serialization is provided per-struct via jsonStringify functions to decouple data representation from transport.

Interaction with rest of system: collectForms is intended to be called by page-level inspection tools (e.g., MCP/CDP tooling, mcp/tools.zig). registerNodes accepts an abstract registry (anytype) so it can operate against different registry implementations (CDP or MCP) and populate backendNodeId fields used by external protocols. The module intentionally defers complex element semantics (e.g., disabled-state computation, input value resolution) to Element/Node/Page APIs: it calls el.isDisabled(), input.getValue(), select.getValue(page) and option.getText(page), so modifications to Element behavior affect form extraction.

Tests: this file includes embedded Zig unit tests that exercise many edge cases (hidden inputs, select/textarea, disabled fieldsets, external fields via form attribute, checkbox/radio value extraction, and default action/method behavior). The tests create a lightweight Page via testing.test_session, parse HTML fragments into a document subtree, and call collectForms to assert expected outcomes.

key_constructs.name: SelectOption
key_constructs.purpose: Represents a single <option> with value and displayed text and can serialize itself to JSON.
key_constructs.reasoning: This small POD struct isolates option serialization logic and stores text/value slices that are borrowed from the arena. Editing must preserve that the fields are slices and that jsonStringify uses jw.write on those slices.

key_constructs.name: FormField
key_constructs.purpose: Holds metadata for a single form-associated control (tag, name, type, required/disabled/value/placeholder/options) and serializes to JSON.
key_constructs.reasoning: FormField is the per-control representation used by callers. Note required/disabled are explicit bools and always emitted in JSON; value and placeholder are optional nullable slices. Any change must respect lifetime semantics of slices and the JSON shape (clients expect presence of required/disabled keys).

key_constructs.name: FormInfo
key_constructs.purpose: Represents a <form> element with action, method, a list of FormField entries and optional backendNodeId for protocol mapping.
key_constructs.reasoning: FormInfo is the aggregate unit that detectForms and other tools consume. It includes fields that may be null. The method/action extraction follows HTML rules (getMethod/getAttributeSafe). Returned slices borrow the arena passed to collectForms.

key_constructs.name: registerNodes
key_constructs.purpose: Registers form and field Node pointers in an external registry and writes back backendNodeId values.
key_constructs.reasoning: This function abstracts over different registry implementations (CDP/MCP). It expects registry.register(node) to return an object with an id field. Changes to registry API or the backendNodeId representation will require coordinated updates.

key_constructs.name: collectForms
key_constructs.purpose: Walks a DOM subtree starting at root, finds <form> elements, gathers their fields and returns an arena-backed slice of FormInfo.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the public entry point for form detection. It requires the caller to provide an arena allocator and a Page pointer. The arena must be an arena allocator because returned slices borrow its memory — changing allocation strategy or lifetimes impacts consumers.

key_constructs.name: collectFormFields
key_constructs.purpose: Given a Form element, returns a list of FormField entries for its form-associated controls, skipping non-fillable/hidden controls.
key_constructs.reasoning: Contains important rules: hidden inputs and input types submit/button/image are omitted; textareas/selects/inputs are handled distinctly; element.isDisabled() is used to determine disabled state. Modifying field inclusion rules should respect existing tests that exercise common edge cases.

key_constructs.name: collectSelectOptions
key_constructs.purpose: Walks descendants of a select node to collect Option elements and their value/text pairs.
key_constructs.reasoning: Returns an arena-backed slice of SelectOption. It uses TreeWalker.Full to find <option> descendants, so changing traversal semantics or TreeWalker behavior will affect output order and content.

key_constructs.name: testForms
key_constructs.purpose: Test helper that creates a Page, injects given HTML into a DIV and returns collectForms output for tests.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used exclusively by embedded tests in this file and depends on testing.test_session and page.parseHtmlAsChildren; any change to tests or testing harness may require updating this helper.

key_behaviors: discovers forms and their associated fields under a DOM root
collects select options and field attributes into arena-backed representations
serializes form structures to JSON for protocol transmission
registers nodes in external registries to assign backend IDs

pitfalls.mistake: Treat the allocator parameter as a general-purpose allocator or a short-lived allocator.
pitfalls.consequence: Returned slices will point to freed memory leading to use-after-free and memory corruption when consumed later.
pitfalls.prevention: Pass an arena allocator with a lifetime that outlives consumers of the returned slices; follow the function's doc comment about arena requirements.

pitfalls.mistake: Change the JSON output shape by conditionally omitting required/disabled keys.
pitfalls.consequence: Consumers expecting explicit boolean fields may break (protocol incompatibility).
pitfalls.prevention: Keep required and disabled booleans always emitted; the code already writes them unconditionally.

pitfalls.mistake: Modify element inclusion rules (e.g., include hidden inputs or buttons) without updating tests and protocol expectations.
pitfalls.consequence: DetectForms and tools (MCP/CDP) will observe unexpected fields; clients relying on previous behavior may break.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect current skipping of hidden inputs and non-fillable buttons and run tests to validate behavior.

pitfalls.mistake: Assume registry.register has a specific return type beyond an object with an `id` field.
pitfalls.consequence: registerNodes will fail at compile-time or runtime if the registry API changes shape.
pitfalls.prevention: When changing registry implementations, keep register(node) returning a structure with an `id` or update registerNodes accordingly.

pitfalls.mistake: Change traversal strategy (TreeWalker usage) without considering external-field (form attribute) semantics.
pitfalls.consequence: Fields that live outside the form but reference it via form="id" may be missed or duplicated.
pitfalls.prevention: Rely on Form.getElements() semantics as present; consult Element.getElements/form.getElements implementations if altering traversal.

key_sections: collectForms: main entry — walks the DOM, calls collectFormFields, documents allocator lifetime
collectFormFields: contains per-control logic (skipping hidden/submit/button/image, handling input/textarea/select)
registerNodes: maps Nodes to backendNodeId using abstract registries
skip_unless_needed: jsonStringify implementations (unless changing JSON format)
embedded tests helper testForms (unless modifying tests or test harness)

test_functions: browser.forms: external field via form attribute
browser.forms: checkbox and radio return value attribute

insights.title: Centralize disabled-state logic into Element.isDisabled
insights.problem: Multiple modules duplicated logic to determine whether an element is disabled (direct disabled attribute and disabled by ancestor fieldset with legend exception).
insights.root_cause: Disabled-state logic was implemented ad-hoc in forms, interactive, FormData and AX generation code leading to duplication and subtle inconsistencies.
insights.solution: Introduce Element.isDisabled() that implements the spec (checks disabled attribute and disabled fieldset ancestor while handling legend exception). Replace various isDisabledByFieldset / ad-hoc checks with calls to el.isDisabled().

insights.title: Serialize required/disabled fields consistently
insights.problem: JSON writer emitted required/disabled only when true (via conditional blocks), producing inconsistent output shape for consumers expecting explicit booleans.
insights.root_cause: Conditional emission produced absent fields instead of explicit false values; consumers couldn't rely on presence of keys.
insights.solution: Always write the required and disabled keys (write the bool value directly) so the property is always present in serialized form.

insights.title: Document arena allocator requirement for collectForms
insights.problem: collectForms returns slices that borrow allocator-managed memory but callers might not realize the requirement.
insights.root_cause: API contract wasn't explicit in the function docs; users could provide wrong allocator kind and cause dangling slices.
insights.solution: Clarify in the doc comment that `arena` must be an arena allocator because returned slices borrow its memory.

insights.title: Tests for checkbox/radio values and form defaults
insights.problem: Missing unit tests for input value behavior (checkbox/radio return value attribute) and default form action/method behavior.
insights.root_cause: Lack of coverage for these HTML form edge cases increased regression risk.
insights.solution: Add tests validating checkbox/radio .value extraction and that a form without action/method yields action == null and method == 'get'.

src/browser/StyleManager.zig.short_description: Manages visibility- and pointer-events-related CSS rules, buckets them for fast selector matching, and evaluates element visibility/pointer-events.
src/browser/StyleManager.zig.description:
This module implements a StyleManager that extracts visibility-relevant CSS from stylesheets and inline styles, buckets rules by the rightmost selector part for efficient matching, and exposes functions to determine whether an Element (or its ancestors) is hidden or has pointer-events:none. It exists to provide a fast, incremental mechanism for visibility/pointer-events checks that are used throughout page-level behavior (e.g., hit-testing, event targeting, DOM dumps). The design emphasizes performance: rules are bucketed (id/class/tag/other) to avoid scanning all rules for each element, and caching is supported via AutoHashMapUnmanaged typed for Elements.

Key design decisions are visible in how rules are represented and prioritized: each VisibilityRule contains a packed u64 priority composed of specificity (high 32 bits) and a document-order counter (low 32 bits). Inline styles are represented as having a unique INLINE_PRIORITY so they win over stylesheet rules. The manager lazily rebuilds its rule sets (rebuildIfDirty) and uses a dedicated allocator arena (self.arena) per StyleManager obtained from the Page. The module integrates with external parsers: CssParser for raw CSS text and SelectorParser to produce selector ASTs; it also consumes CSSStyleRule objects when stylesheets are already parsed.

The file interacts with Page (for arenas, document stylesheets, and session-managed arena pools), Element (selector matching, getting attributes, inline style lookup), CSSStyleSheet/CSSStyleRule (stylesheet-backed rules), Selector/SelectorParser (selector ASTs), and Selector matching helpers (matchesSelector, computeSpecificity, getBucketKey, getInlineStyleProperty). It performs error handling and logs parse errors back to a log module. Careful lifetime management of arena allocations, and correct preservation of the dirty flag across errors (errdefer) are important behavioral aspects.

key_constructs.name: VisibilityCache
key_constructs.purpose: Type alias for a hashmap caching visibility results per Element.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to memoize visibility results while walking ancestors to avoid repeated expensive recomputation.

key_constructs.name: PointerEventsCache
key_constructs.purpose: Type alias for a hashmap caching pointer-events:none checks per Element.
key_constructs.reasoning: Analogous to VisibilityCache; caches pointer-events results for ancestor traversal and performance.

key_constructs.name: StyleManager
key_constructs.purpose: Primary struct representing the style manager instance attached to a Page.
key_constructs.reasoning: Central entry point for building, storing, and querying visibility/pointer-events rules and caches. Contains rule buckets, allocator, and dirty flag.

key_constructs.name: init
key_constructs.purpose: Constructs and initializes a StyleManager for a given Page, acquiring an allocator arena.
key_constructs.reasoning: Entry point for creating the manager; callers must respect returned arena ownership and call deinit on teardown.

key_constructs.name: deinit
key_constructs.purpose: Releases the arena allocated to the StyleManager.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures memory allocated from the Page's arena pool is released; must be called when the manager is destroyed.

key_constructs.name: parseSheet
key_constructs.purpose: Parses a CSSStyleSheet and extracts visibility-related rules from it (either via pre-parsed rules or raw <style> text).
key_constructs.reasoning: Handles both stylesheet objects that already have CSSRule objects and inline <style> nodes whose text must be parsed with CssParser; errors are logged and bubbled as appropriate.

key_constructs.name: addRawRule
key_constructs.purpose: Parses a single selector text and declaration block into visibility properties and creates VisibilityRule entries into buckets.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used when parsing raw CSS text; relies on SelectorParser and only records rules that affect display/visibility/opacity/pointer-events.

key_constructs.name: sheetRemoved
key_constructs.purpose: Marks the rule set dirty when a stylesheet is removed.
key_constructs.reasoning: Triggers lazy rebuild on next query; callers (stylesheet lifecycle code) must call this so the manager invalidates caches.

key_constructs.name: sheetModified
key_constructs.purpose: Marks the rule set dirty when a stylesheet is modified.
key_constructs.reasoning: Same role as sheetRemoved; used by stylesheet mutation code to force a rebuild on the next visibility query.

key_constructs.name: rebuildIfDirty
key_constructs.purpose: Lazily rebuilds all rule buckets from the document's stylesheets when the dirty flag is set.
key_constructs.reasoning: Resets internal data structures and re-parses stylesheets; important to respect allocator lifetimes, counts for ensureTotalCapacity, and the errdefer that resets dirty on error.

key_constructs.name: isHidden
key_constructs.purpose: Public API to determine if an element or any ancestor is hidden under the configured options, with optional caching.
key_constructs.reasoning: Walks up ancestors, consults optional VisibilityCache, calls isElementHidden for per-element evaluation, and stores results back into the cache using page.call_arena.

key_constructs.name: isElementHidden
key_constructs.purpose: Evaluates whether a single element is hidden by display/visibility/opacity, considering inline styles and stylesheet rules with specificity/priority.
key_constructs.reasoning: Performs the core rule-matching and priority comparison logic; uses an inner Ctx to iterate appropriate rule buckets and update best-matching property values.

key_constructs.name: hasPointerEventsNone
key_constructs.purpose: Public API to determine whether an element (or its ancestors) has pointer-events:none, with optional caching.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ancestor-walking equivalent to isHidden but specialized for pointer-events; uses cache similarly and defers per-element logic to elementHasPointerEventsNone.

key_constructs.name: elementHasPointerEventsNone
key_constructs.purpose: Checks pointer-events:none for a single element, respecting inline style precedence and stylesheet rules with specificity/priority.
key_constructs.reasoning: Mirrors isElementHidden's priority mechanics but focuses only on pointer-events; uses a small helper checkRules closure to iterate candidate rules.

key_constructs.name: addRule
key_constructs.purpose: Converts a CSSStyleRule (already parsed) into one or more VisibilityRule entries and places them in the appropriate buckets.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used when stylesheet objects expose CSSRule entries; similar semantic role to addRawRule but consumes CSSStyleRule structures.

key_constructs.name: Tag
key_constructs.purpose: Alias for Element.Tag used as key type in tag_rules bucket.
key_constructs.reasoning: Helps bucket tag-based rules using Element.Tag enumeration/identifier.

key_behaviors: rebuilds rule list lazily when stylesheets change
parses stylesheet text and CSSStyleRule objects into visibility-specific rules
buckets rules by rightmost selector part for fast lookup
evaluates element visibility and pointer-events with specificity+doc-order priority
supports caching of per-element checks during ancestor traversal

pitfalls.mistake: Calling rebuildIfDirty and ignoring the return error
pitfalls.consequence: The function returns early on error and callers like isHidden/hasPointerEventsNone currently catch and return false; silently swallowing errors may mask parse issues.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect that rebuildIfDirty can fail; understand that callers intentionally treat failures as "not hidden" or "no pointer-events:none" and adjust callers accordingly if behavior changes.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying the packed priority encoding (computeSpecificity << 32 | doc_order) without updating comparisons
pitfalls.consequence: Breaking specificity-vs-doc-order tie-breaking will change which rules win; selector matching and test outcomes will diverge.
pitfalls.prevention: Any edits affecting priority packing must preserve the shifting and comparisons semantics used throughout isElementHidden and elementHasPointerEventsNone.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing arena allocation or lifetime semantics (self.arena, page.getArena, page.releaseArena, resetRetain) without accounting for retained pointers
pitfalls.consequence: Memory corruption, use-after-free, or allocator leaks when buckets or selector objects reference buffers from freed arenas.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect that StyleManager stores selectors and rule data into self.arena and that rebuildIfDirty calls page._session.arena_pool.resetRetain(self.arena); do not free or reuse arenas unexpectedly.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering cache keying or use of Element pointers (VisibilityCache/PointerEventsCache) to something non-pointer-based
pitfalls.consequence: Cached entries may become invalid or fail to match, causing inconsistent results or missed cache hits.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep caches keyed by Element pointer identity as callers (and other modules) expect pointer-based equality.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the behavior of inline style precedence (INLINE_PRIORITY)
pitfalls.consequence: Inline styles may no longer reliably override stylesheet rules, altering visibility/pointer-events decisions throughout the runtime.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve INLINE_PRIORITY semantics or update all comparisons and callers consistently when changing it.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming SelectorParser.parseList or CssParser.parseDeclarationsList always succeed
pitfalls.consequence: Parser failures are possible and the code contains guards (catch return / logging). Ignoring this can introduce unhandled errors.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep existing error handling (catch, logging). If changing parsing flows, ensure errors are handled in the same fault-tolerant manner.

key_sections: rebuildIfDirty: shows lazy rebuild, arena resetRetain, bucket reallocation, and parsing loop over document stylesheets
addRawRule / addRule: conversion from raw CSS or CSSStyleRule into VisibilityRule and bucketing logic
isHidden / isElementHidden: core matching and priority logic used at runtime
hasPointerEventsNone / elementHasPointerEventsNone: specialized pointer-events logic
skip_unless_needed: internal small helper closures/structs inside isElementHidden (Ctx) and elementHasPointerEventsNone (checkRules) — read only when changing matching/priority logic
detailed parsing/tokenization loops unless working on selector parsing or class tokenization

tests.example_command: zig build test (or make test) to run project unit tests that may exercise StyleManager indirectly

related_files.relationship: StyleManager is instantiated per Page; uses Page for allocator arenas, document style-sheets, session arena_pool, and page-level helpers.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to Page arena API, document._style_sheets shape, or how inline styles are exposed will affect StyleManager initialization and rebuild semantics.

related_files.relationship: Element provides APIs used by StyleManager: getAttributeSafe, parentElement(), getTag(), inline style accessors and getTextContentAlloc used by parse pathways.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change in Element attribute APIs, inline-style storage, or ownership/lifetimes impacts matching and cache usage.

related_files.relationship: StyleManager consumes CSSStyleRule instances (addRule) and their _selector_text/_style/_style declarations.
related_files.reason_to_check: Modifications to CSSStyleRule internals or property names change how rules are extracted and must be synchronized here.

related_files.relationship: SelectorParser.parseList is used to build selector ASTs for rule bucketing and matching.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to selector AST shape (segments, compound, first) or parse API will require adapting bucket-key logic and matching calls.

related_files.relationship: CssParser.parseStylesheet and parseDeclarationsList are used to parse raw <style> contents or raw blocks when CSSStyleRule objects are not present.
related_files.reason_to_check: Parser API changes or different tokenization behavior can alter which declarations are considered visibility-relevant.

data_constants_to_check: priority packing/bit-shift constants used to encode specificity/doc-order

src/Server.zig.short_description: TCP/CDP server: accepts HTTP requests, upgrades to WebSocket/CDP sessions, and manages client lifecycle and threading.
src/Server.zig.description:
This file implements the server component that accepts incoming TCP connections, serves a minimal HTTP endpoint for CDP discovery, performs WebSocket upgrades, and manages per-connection CDP (Chrome DevTools Protocol) sessions. It ties together higher-level components (App, configuration) with lower-level I/O and protocol modules (network/websocket.zig, browser/HttpClient.zig, cdp/CDP.zig). The Server allocates and recycles Client instances via a memory pool, enforces a maximum concurrent connection limit using an atomic active_threads counter, and spawns detached worker threads for each accepted connection.

The Client inner struct is the per-connection handler. It begins in HTTP mode to accept GET requests (only a tiny subset: root path for websocket upgrade and /json/version to advertise the websocket URL) and, on upgrade, transitions to .cdp mode which initializes a CDP object and forwards messages between the websocket and the CDP runtime. The design separates HTTP handling (HttpClient) from WebSocket/CDP handling (WsConnection + CDP), and uses a union enum for the mode to make the lifecycle explicit. The file contains careful shutdown semantics, mutex-protected client lists, and an explicit joinThreads helper to wait for worker threads to finish during deinit.

There are a number of non-obvious design decisions embedded here: the Client is allocated on the heap via a MemoryPool because of its large stack usage (noted in a comment about V8 crashes if kept on the stack); /json/version requests are intentionally answered with a Connection: Close header and the socket is closed to avoid interoperability issues with chromedp (Go driver) that sometimes issues the upgrade on a second connection; active thread counting uses a cmpxchgWeak CAS loop to atomically ensure the maxConnections limit is respected even under races; and the HTTP request handling enforces strict size limits and structure (no bodies, GET only, CRLF-terminated), returning explicit HTTP error responses for various malformed cases.

key_constructs.name: init
key_constructs.purpose: Create and initialize a Server instance, bind the accept socket and prepare json_version_response.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the main constructor connecting the Server with App and network; it must build the JSON discovery payload and bind the listening socket before returning the Server reference.

key_constructs.name: shutdown
key_constructs.purpose: Signal all registered clients to stop and perform orderly shutdown actions.
key_constructs.reasoning: Iterates registered clients under a mutex and calls stop() on each. Ordering matters relative to registerClient/unregisterClient and deinit to avoid races where newly accepted clients may not receive a stop() call.

key_constructs.name: deinit
key_constructs.purpose: Full teardown: calls shutdown, waits for worker threads, deinitializes client list and pool, frees json response and destroys the Server allocation.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures no lingering threads/clients hold resources or reference freed memory; the sequence (shutdown -> joinThreads -> deinit pools -> free response -> destroy) must be preserved to avoid use-after-free.

key_constructs.name: spawnWorker
key_constructs.purpose: Atomically checks max connections and spawns a detached worker thread to handle one accepted socket.
key_constructs.reasoning: Correctly enforces the maxConnections constraint using an atomic compare-and-exchange (weak) CAS loop with retry semantics. Decrements active_threads on error via errdefer to avoid leaking active count.

key_constructs.name: runWorker
key_constructs.purpose: Thread entry that decrements the active thread counter when the worker finishes and then calls handleConnection.
key_constructs.reasoning: Must ensure the active_threads counter is decremented exactly once when a worker exits; it does so with a defer that fetchSub()s the counter.

key_constructs.name: Client
key_constructs.purpose: Per-connection struct handling HTTP parsing, websocket upgrade, CDP initialization and message routing.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates connection state and lifecycle. The union enum mode(.http or .cdp) makes transitions explicit and drives behavior in processData(), httpLoop(), and the stop/deinit routines.

key_constructs.name: Client.init
key_constructs.purpose: Initialize per-client WsConnection and HttpClient, log client address, and return a Client instance.
key_constructs.reasoning: Allocates and sets up both the websocket connection wrapper and HTTP client. Uses errdefer to ensure partially-initialized resources are cleaned on failure.

key_constructs.name: Client.httpLoop
key_constructs.purpose: Drive the initial HTTP handshake loop until a websocket upgrade occurs or a timeout happens.
key_constructs.reasoning: Runs http.tick and reads socket data until either the connection is upgraded to CDP or a timeout/close occurs. Handles the interleaving of HTTP ticks and socket reads required for the server's single-socket-per-client flow.

key_constructs.name: processHTTPRequest
key_constructs.purpose: Parse a complete HTTP GET request from the client's buffer and route it (upgrade, /json/version, or error).
key_constructs.reasoning: Performs strict validation: minimum size, request method 'GET', URL parsing, CRLF termination and size threshold enforcement (Config.CDP_MAX_HTTP_REQUEST_SIZE). It returns precise error codes used to write appropriate HTTP error responses.

key_constructs.name: upgradeConnection
key_constructs.purpose: Perform the websocket upgrade and initialize the CDP mode.
key_constructs.reasoning: Delegates the heavy-lifting to ws.upgrade(request) then sets client.mode to .cdp with CDP.init(self); this transition is critical and subsequent call paths assume mode is correct.

key_constructs.name: buildJSONVersionResponse
key_constructs.purpose: Prepare the HTTP response payload for /json/version, including a Connection: Close header and the webSocketDebuggerUrl value.
key_constructs.reasoning: This response is returned immediately and the socket is closed to avoid chromedp's two-connection flow blocking when the server is limited to 1 connection.

key_behaviors: accepts TCP connections and binds to the configured address
parses minimal HTTP GET requests and enforces strict request size/format limits
upgrades HTTP connections to WebSocket and initializes a CDP session
enforces maximum concurrent connections via atomic active_threads and memory pool
coordinates clean shutdown of clients and worker threads

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying the active_threads increment/decrement logic or removing the errdefer that decrements the count on spawn failure.
pitfalls.consequence: Can leak active thread count leading to permanent refusal of new connections or server hang during joinThreads() at shutdown.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the current CAS loop and the errdefer decrement semantics when editing spawnWorker/runWorker; ensure active_threads is decremented exactly once in all paths.

pitfalls.mistake: Allocating Client as a stack local (or shrinking the heap allocation strategy) instead of using the MemoryPool approach.
pitfalls.consequence: The comment documents V8 crashes when the client is on the stack due to its large size (>512KB). Changing allocation strategy may reintroduce crashes.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve heap allocation for the Client and the MemoryPool lifecycle (create/destroy) under the client_mutex.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the order of registerClient and the subsequent check of app.shutdown() in handleConnection.
pitfalls.consequence: If registerClient is done after the shutdown check, deinit() might iterate over clients and call stop() before this client is registered and thus this client would never get stop(), causing joinThreads() to block indefinitely.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep registerClient() before the shutdown check in handleConnection to ensure every accepted client can receive a stop() call during Server.shutdown().

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming the result of pageWait or similar wait APIs cannot return a normal .done completion (treating it as unreachable).
pitfalls.consequence: Past commits fixed a bug where .done was treated as unreachable; not handling .done now can lead to uncontrolled behavior or incorrect logging on timeouts.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the commit history: handle .done explicitly in loops that wait for CDP results and treat it as a timeout-like completion where applicable.

pitfalls.mistake: Editing processHTTPRequest parsing (e.g., endsWith CRLF check, URL detection) without considering strictness about bodies and methods.
pitfalls.consequence: Could allow unexpected requests, break the Chromedp interaction assumptions, or mis-handle /json/version which is expected to be handled then closed.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep strict GET-only, CRLF-terminated behavior and the size limit via Config.CDP_MAX_HTTP_REQUEST_SIZE unless coordinating a larger protocol change across components.

key_sections: spawnWorker: enforces max connections and spawns worker threads (concurrency model)
runWorker/handleConnection: worker entry and per-connection lifecycle
Client.start/httpLoop/processHTTPRequest: the HTTP-to-WebSocket upgrade flow and how sockets are read
Client.stop/deinit and Server.shutdown/deinit: shutdown ordering and resource cleanup
skip_unless_needed: low-level log statements and small helpers such as writeHTTPErrorResponse
the exact formatting constants inside buildJSONVersionResponse unless altering advertised payloads

tests.example_command: make test (or zig build test) to run the repository tests; none specific to this file are present in the provided context.

related_files.relationship: Server uses App for allocator, config and shutdown state; changes to App.config or shutdown semantics affect Server behavior.
related_files.reason_to_check: If modifying maxConnections, timeout logic or shutdown signaling, inspect App.zig to ensure config APIs and shutdown semantics align.

related_files.relationship: Client upgrades to CDP and delegates message handling to CDP; changes to CDP API or pageWait semantics affect Client loops.
related_files.reason_to_check: Adjustments to CDP.wait or CDP.init must be mirrored here (e.g., handling of .done vs error cases).

related_files.relationship: WsConnection provides socket reading/upgrade/send primitives used throughout Client; Server depends on its buffer/reader semantics.
related_files.reason_to_check: If websocket buffering, blocking read semantics, or shutdown behavior change, the Client.httpLoop/readSocket logic may need to be adjusted.

related_files.relationship: HttpClient.tick and cdp_client callbacks are used to interleave HTTP ticks and blocking reads during handshake and CDP runtime.
related_files.reason_to_check: Alterations to HttpClient.tick return semantics or cdp_client callback behaviors must be reconciled with Client.httpLoop and blockingRead* functions.

related_files.relationship: Defines constants such as CDP_MAX_HTTP_REQUEST_SIZE and provides maxConnections() and cdpTimeout(); Server uses these values extensively.
related_files.reason_to_check: If changing limits or default timeouts, ensure the Server enforces and reports them correctly; tests and behavior (e.g., /json/version close) depend on these.

insights.title: Handle CDPWaitResult.done branch instead of unreachable
insights.problem: Server code treated the .done branch of a wait result as unreachable which could cause incorrect behavior/logging.
insights.root_cause: Wait logic evolution changed return semantics; unreachable was incorrect for a timeout-equivalent completion.
insights.solution: Handle .done branch: log a CDP timeout and return instead of unreachable.
insights.lesson_learned: When wait/timeout APIs change to return non-error completion enums, handle the .done case explicitly instead of assuming unreachable.

insights.title: Fix CDP inactivity timeout when browser is busy
insights.problem: Client loop timed out even if the browser had active background work (HTTP requests / macrotasks).
insights.root_cause: The inactivity timeout counter wasn't reset when the system was busy, so the server could close an active session incorrectly.
insights.solution: Move timeout and busy logic into Runner (commit 1770dc0), and earlier patch added isBusy/timeout reset logic in Server to avoid premature timeouts (commits fffa8b6, 1770dc0).
insights.lesson_learned: Timeout logic must account for asynchronous activity both in networking and in VM task queues; centralize wait semantics into Runner for consistency.

src/main.zig.short_description: CLI entrypoint: parse args, initialize global app, and dispatch serve/fetch/mcp modes
src/main.zig.description:
This file is the program entry point for the Lightpanda binary. It exists to: set up the top-level allocator policy (Debug vs Release), create a short-lived arena for main-specific allocations, parse CLI arguments, install the global signal handler, instantiate the global App object (which owns the browser runtime), and dispatch to one of the supported run modes (serve, fetch, mcp). The run modes branch into concrete runtime paths: starting a CDP server (serve), performing a single fetch+dumppipeline (fetch), or running an MCP (management/control) worker possibly alongside an optional local CDP server (mcp).

The file encodes lifecycle rules and ordering constraints that other modules rely on: the signal handler must be installed before spawning any other threads, the global App is constructed once and deferred for deinit to support graceful shutdown, and app.network.run() is the main event loop that blocks until the process is signaled to stop. It also wires CLI options into runtime structures such as logging options and FetchOpts. Some runtime behaviors are intentionally guarded: AddressInUse is handled specially during server startup and certain fetch options (wait_ms, wait_until, wait_script, wait_selector, dump options) are forwarded into lp.FetchOpts.

This file imports and composes higher-level library modules (lp.App, lp.Server, lp.fetch, lp.mcp.Server) and lower-level utilities (SigHandler), so edits must respect the contracts of those modules. The commit history visible in the repository shows that MCP mode was extended to optionally expose a CDP port, and fetch wait options were propagated from CLI into FetchOpts — both are reflected in the serve/mcp/fetch branches and should be considered authoritative behaviors when modifying the dispatcher or argument/option wiring.

key_constructs.name: panic
key_constructs.purpose: Exports the crash handler panic function used by the binary (pub const panic = lp.crash_handler.panic).
key_constructs.reasoning: This symbol re-exports the crash handler from lp so other modules and the runtime use a centralized panic handler. Maintain its signature and semantics because it establishes the program-wide panic behaviour.

key_constructs.name: main
key_constructs.purpose: Top-level entrypoint that sets up allocators, arena, and calls run(); handles fatal errors by logging and exiting.
key_constructs.reasoning: All process initialization (Debug vs Release allocator selection, arena lifecycle, leak detection in Debug) begins here. Any modification to allocator choice, leak detection, or process exit paths here affects memory diagnostics and shutdown behavior globally.

key_constructs.name: run
key_constructs.purpose: Parses CLI args, applies logging options, installs the SigHandler, initializes the App, and dispatches to serve/fetch/mcp modes.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the central dispatcher for command-line modes. It wires CLI options to runtime structures (FetchOpts, server address parsing), handles error-specific logging (e.g., AddressInUse), and registers shutdown hooks. Changes here impact mode behavior and how subsystems are started/stopped.

key_constructs.name: fetchThread
key_constructs.purpose: Thread entry point used for the fetch mode worker: calls lp.fetch and ensures app.network.stop() is called on completion.
key_constructs.reasoning: fetchThread encapsulates the single-run fetch lifecycle in a spawned thread and guarantees network stop via defer. Thread creation/joining semantics in run() expect this shape — altering it changes fetch concurrency and shutdown semantics.

key_constructs.name: mcpThread
key_constructs.purpose: Thread entry point for MCP mode: initializes lp.mcp.Server with provided allocator, processes requests from stdin, and ensures app.network.stop() on exit.
key_constructs.reasoning: mcpThread is responsible for starting and running the MCP request loop and for integrating an optional local CDP server. It uses a stdout writer and a stdin buffer to process requests synchronously. Changes affect how MCP and optional CDP coexist and how stdin/stdout are consumed.

key_behaviors: parses command-line arguments and applies them to runtime configuration
installs global signal handlers before spawning threads
initializes global App and defers its deinitialization for graceful shutdown
starts the CDP server when in serve mode or when --cdp-port is specified in mcp mode
spawns worker threads for fetch and mcp modes and joins them synchronously
runs the network event loop via app.network.run() until shutdown

pitfalls.mistake: Install the SigHandler after spawning threads or initializing subsystems that create threads.
pitfalls.consequence: Signals (SIGINT/SIGTERM) may not be handled as expected, causing improper shutdown, leaked resources, or raced deinitialization.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the ordering: create and install SigHandler before starting other threads or subsystems; preserve sighandler.on registrations.

pitfalls.mistake: Change allocator selection or remove debug leak detection instrumentation without considering Debug vs Release flows.
pitfalls.consequence: Debug-mode leak detection will not run or may misreport; memory diagnostics relied on in CI/locally will be altered, causing harder-to-debug memory issues.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the builtin.mode conditional logic selecting std.heap.DebugAllocator in Debug and std.heap.c_allocator in Release; if modifying, ensure equivalent leak-detection and allocator behavior is preserved.

pitfalls.mistake: Alter the order of app.deinit() defer relative to sighandler.on registrations or server deinitialization.
pitfalls.consequence: Resources might be deinitialized in the wrong order (for example network being stopped before server deinit), creating panics or crashes during shutdown.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain current defer ordering: server deinit and other registered shutdown resources should be paired with sighandler.on and App.deinit left at top-level defer to ensure consistent lifecycle.

pitfalls.mistake: Start the local CDP server in mcp mode without registering it with the sighandler for shutdown.
pitfalls.consequence: The CDP server may persist after network stop, leading to hung processes or port conflicts on subsequent runs.
pitfalls.prevention: When initializing cdp_server in mcp branch, ensure sighandler.on is called as currently implemented and defer s.deinit() is executed.

key_sections: run: command-line parsing and main dispatcher (serve/fetch/mcp) — central to process behavior
serve branch: server initialization, address parsing, handling AddressInUse error — important for CDP start-up
fetch branch: construction of lp.FetchOpts and thread spawning — shows how CLI fetch flags map to runtime
mcp branch: optional cdp_port handling and mcpThread spawn — explains MCP + optional CDP coexistence
skip_unless_needed: allocator debug/release plumbing at the top of main (unless working on memory/leak issues)
the small writer/stdin buffer ergonomics inside mcpThread (unless editing MCP IO)

tests.example_command: Run unit tests and build targets to validate changes: `zig build test` and `make build` (project-level commands). To run the CLI manually: `./zig-out/bin/lightpanda serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 9222`

related_files.relationship: Provides the App, Server and other lp.* modules used here; this file composes those high-level runtime primitives into the CLI behavior.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to App.init/deinit, Server.init contract or snapshot APIs will require corresponding updates here (argument order, error handling, shutdown hooks).

related_files.relationship: Defines lp.mcp.Server and router processing used by mcpThread; mcp mode in main.zig initializes and delegates stdin processing to that module.
related_files.reason_to_check: If mcp.Server.init or router.processRequests signatures change, mcpThread must be updated accordingly; if mcp gains asynchronous IO semantics, the thread model here may need adaptation.

related_files.relationship: Provides the SigHandler type that's instantiated and installed early in main; used to register shutdown callbacks for subsystems (network, servers).
related_files.reason_to_check: Modifying SigHandler API (install, on) affects ordering and registration logic in run().

related_files.relationship: Build-time options (snapshot_path, prebuilt_v8_path, optimize flags) influence main runtime behavior (app.snapshot.fromEmbedded() visibility and snapshot presence).
related_files.reason_to_check: If snapshot generation or embedding changes, the informational log lines and any assumptions about app.snapshot.fromEmbedded() in logs might need to be reconciled.

tests_to_run: run server locally: ./zig-out/bin/lightpanda serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 9222 to validate startup/shutdown
data_constants_to_check: FetchOpts fields (wait_ms, wait_until, wait_script, wait_selector, dump options) to ensure CLI mapping remains consistent
CDP port and address parsing (std.net.Address.parseIp usage) and error handling (AddressInUse)
snapshot embedding reference (app.snapshot.fromEmbedded()) and build.zig snapshot_path if related

insights.title: CDP server option for MCP mode
insights.problem: MCP mode previously couldn't expose a CDP port alongside tools.
insights.root_cause: No configuration to start a CDP server while running the MCP worker.
insights.solution: Add --cdp-port option and initialize an lp.Server CDP server in MCP mode when specified; ensure proper shutdown registration (commit 58fc60d).
insights.lesson_learned: When building multi-mode servers, allow optional subsystems (like CDP) to be enabled via config and cleanly integrated into lifecycle management.

insights.title: Propagate new fetch wait options from CLI into FetchOpts
insights.problem: New CLI fetch options needed to be forwarded into the runtime FetchOpts.
insights.root_cause: Missing wiring between CLI parsing and runtime fetch invocation.
insights.solution: Wire wait_script and wait_selector into FetchOpts when invoking lightpanda.fetch (commit ab6c63b).
insights.lesson_learned: When extending CLI options, ensure they are consistently threaded into the runtime call path and documented in usage text.

src/testing.zig.short_description: Test harness and helper utilities for Zig-implemented browser runtime unit and web-API tests
src/testing.zig.description:
This file provides the central test harness used across the repository's Zig unit tests and the web-api style tests that drive the embedded JS runtime + DOM. It exposes reusable testing utilities (assertions, JSON comparators, arena allocator, random helpers), manages test-global fixtures (test_app, test_session, test_http/test_ws servers, test_browser, test_config), and implements higher-level helpers to run web-api HTML tests (htmlRunner, runWebApiTest, pageTest). The helpers coordinate Zig-side runner ticks with the embedded JS microtask/promise lifecycle so tests that spawn asynchronous JS work can reliably complete or time out.

Design choices visible in this file include: using the std.testing global allocator as the default allocator; providing a per-test arena allocator (arena_instance) for cheap ephemeral allocations; providing merged, flexible equality helpers (expectEqual, expectDelta) that handle many Zig types including arrays, pointers, optionals, structs and tagged unions; and JSON-oriented helpers (convertToJson, expectJson, isEqualJson) to compare complex structures accurately. The web-api runner logic demonstrates explicit coordination with the Session runner: it navigates pages via a test_session, enters a JS local scope, executes testing.assertOk() in JS repeatedly while periodically calling runner.tick and sleeping until success or timeout. Test server management variables and beforeAll/afterAll style Zig tests live in this file (the file contains a test block tests:beforeAll), so it both defines helpers and drives test lifecycle.

This file is tightly coupled with many runtime modules: App.zig, Browser/Page/Session/ScriptManager interfaces (js.zig), HttpClient.zig, Server.zig and the small test HTTP/WebSocket server modules. When editing, the agent must respect the lifecycle and synchronization semantics (arena allocators, thread/server start/stop, session runner tick loop) because many tests depend on subtle timing and resource limits (e.g., ws_max_concurrent override in tests). Commit history indicates this harness was evolved to support async JS tests and to increase default websocket concurrency for test runs, so changes here can affect broad test flakiness or false failures.

key_constructs.name: allocator
key_constructs.purpose: Default allocator used in tests (alias to std.testing.allocator).
key_constructs.reasoning: Many test helpers allocate memory and rely on the test harness allocator for deterministic lifetime and tracking; altering this would change allocation behavior across tests.

key_constructs.name: arena_instance
key_constructs.purpose: An ArenaAllocator used as a cheap test-local ephemeral allocator.
key_constructs.reasoning: Tests use arena_allocator to quickly create mutable strings and buffers without complex lifetime management; its reset() behavior is relied upon between tests.

key_constructs.name: arena_allocator
key_constructs.purpose: The allocator handle derived from arena_instance for use throughout test helpers.
key_constructs.reasoning: Helpers (e.g., newString, htmlRunner path joins) use this allocator for transient buffers; tests assume small, fast allocations returned to the arena.

key_constructs.name: reset
key_constructs.purpose: Reset the arena allocator (retain capacity) to reuse memory between tests.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by htmlRunner and other helpers to ensure a deterministic arena state; modifications must preserve retain_capacity semantics to avoid surprising memory growth or leaks.

key_constructs.name: expectEqual
key_constructs.purpose: Flexible equality assertion that handles strings, arrays, structs, optionals and tagged unions.
key_constructs.reasoning: Many tests call expectEqual with heterogeneous types; the function includes special-case logic (string arrays, optional handling, struct field-by-field comparison, union tagged dispatch). Changing branching or tag handling could break lots of assertions.

key_constructs.name: expectDelta
key_constructs.purpose: Assertion helper to check numeric values are within a delta tolerance.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used for tests where minor numeric deviation is acceptable; it handles optional values and returns error.NotWithinDelta when the assertion fails.

key_constructs.name: Random
key_constructs.purpose: Namespace providing deterministic random helpers and a seeded PRNG initialized from getrandom.
key_constructs.reasoning: Some tests need randomness (fill, fillAtLeast, intRange). The PRNG initialization relies on std.posix.getrandom and stores an instance; altering seeding semantics affects test determinism and reproducibility.

key_constructs.name: expectJson
key_constructs.purpose: Compare two arbitrary values by converting to std.json.Value and asserting structural equality.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to compare complex nested structures in tests; it creates a temporary arena and prints both JSON values on error via errdefer. Conversions use std.json.Stringify and parseFromSliceLeaky.

key_constructs.name: isEqualJson
key_constructs.purpose: Boolean check for structural JSON equality without printing diagnostics.
key_constructs.reasoning: Useful to quickly verify equality in conditional contexts; uses temporary arena for conversions and relies on isJsonValue for deep comparison.

key_constructs.name: htmlRunner
key_constructs.purpose: Top-level helper that runs a web-api HTML test file or all .html files in a directory under WEB_API_TEST_ROOT.
key_constructs.reasoning: Coordinates locating test files, optional per-file std.root.shouldRun filtering, invoking @import("root").subtest and running runWebApiTest for each selected file; respects test filtering and file/directory handling.

key_constructs.name: runWebApiTest
key_constructs.purpose: Execute a single web-api test: create a Page, navigate to local test HTTP server URL, tick the JS runner and poll testing.assertOk() until true or timeout.
key_constructs.reasoning: Central to web-api test execution. It binds a JS Local scope, uses TryCatch to surface JS errors with diagnostics, runs page.navigate, then loops ticking the session runner and executing testing.assertOk() to observe JS-side test completion. Timing, runner.tick semantics and exception handling are critical to avoid spurious timeouts.

key_constructs.name: pageTest
key_constructs.purpose: Create and navigate a page for tests and optionally wait for the runner to quiesce.
key_constructs.reasoning: A convenience wrapper used by unit tests to obtain a navigated *Page. It accepts PageTestOpts.wait_until_done to allow skipping the wait for tests that want to manage timing explicitly.

key_constructs.name: htmlRunnerOpts
key_constructs.purpose: Placeholder options struct for htmlRunner (currently empty).
key_constructs.reasoning: Present for future extension; htmlRunner currently ignores opts but preserves API shape in tests.

key_behaviors: provides flexible assertion helpers for many Zig types
runs HTML-based web-api tests by creating pages and navigating to local HTTP server
coordinates JS runtime microtasks and host runner ticks to wait for async test completion
manages per-test arena allocator and ephemeral allocations

pitfalls.mistake: Replacing or removing the arena_instance/reset behavior
pitfalls.consequence: Tests will allocate more memory or leak across test runs; many helpers depend on quick transient allocations from the arena.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep reset logic; if changing semantics ensure tests still call reset between test cases and retain_capacity semantics preserved.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering expectEqual's special-case branches (strings, arrays, unions, optionals) or union tag handling
pitfalls.consequence: Numerous tests using heterogeneous equality checks will start failing or raise compile-time errors for tagged union comparisons.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the existing branching logic; if adding new type cases, preserve current flows and ensure backwards compatibility.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing Random seeding or switching to a fixed seed without coordinating tests
pitfalls.consequence: Tests that implicitly rely on non-deterministic behavior could behave differently; reproducibility assumptions may be broken.
pitfalls.prevention: If modifying seed behavior, run full test suite to verify no tests rely on previous randomness behavior; prefer explicit seeded tests where determinism is required.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying runWebApiTest timing, runner.tick logic, or the testing.assertOk() polling loop without preserving timeout semantics
pitfalls.consequence: Tests that evolved to rely on the loop (including new testing.async patterns) may become flaky, hang, or time out spuriously.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the loop: call session.runner.tick, execute JS testing.assertOk(), sleep for small intervals, and maintain the existing timeout and error diagnostics.

pitfalls.mistake: Stopping or reconfiguring test server threads (test_http_server/test_ws_server/test_cdp_server) incorrectly
pitfalls.consequence: Server lifecycle mismatches can leave background threads running, causing port conflicts or test interference.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect start/stop patterns used in tests: tests:beforeAll and corresponding teardown code exist (file contains a tests:beforeAll block); ensure threads are joined/cleaned up.

reading_guide.start_here: tests:beforeAll (the Zig test block responsible for global test fixture setup) or runWebApiTest
key_sections: expectEqual: multi-type assertion logic is central for many tests
expectJson / isEqualJson: JSON conversion and deep equality checks for complex structures
runWebApiTest: the polling/tick loop that coordinates Zig and JS runtimes for web-api tests
pageTest / htmlRunner: convenience helpers to create navigated pages and run directories of tests
skip_unless_needed: low-level type trait helpers (is, isPtrTo, isSingleItemPtr) unless you're adding specific type cases
print wrapper (thin wrapper over std.debug.print)

exercised_by: src/browser/tests/testing.js (JS side helpers that call testing.async / testing.assertOk)
Zig unit tests that import testing.zig (includes test blocks such as tests:beforeAll)
test_functions: the Zig test block named "tests:beforeAll" (defined in this file) sets up global fixtures
web-api tests that call testing.async() and expect testing.assertOk() to become true
pageTest consumers in browser unit tests that create and navigate pages
tests.example_command: make test  (project wrapper) or zig build test  (run Zig test suite)

relevant_snippets.description: Top-level test helpers: allocator, arena, expectEqual, expectJson, Random and their behaviors.
htmlRunner and runWebApiTest, including the polling loop that executes testing.assertOk() and runner.tick.
